sessions.intendedAudience: Anyone that is interested in AI, programming for old machine, and/or the combination of them.
sessions.abstract:
What happens when a modern AI coding assistant meets decades-old hardware—and today’s game engines?

In this talk, I share how I’ve used Claude Code as a creative and technical sidekick to build games across radically different platforms: from low-level development on the Commodore 64 and Amiga, to modern workflows in Unity.

I genuinely enjoy writing code—even Assembly—and don’t see AI as a replacement for that craft. Instead, I use it to remove friction: speeding up iteration, navigating obscure hardware constraints, and offloading the tedious parts so I can focus on what’s actually fun.

Working in Assembly, C, and C#, Claude Code has proven surprisingly effective—not just for generating code, but for navigating obscure systems and enabling rapid creation of custom tools—asset pipelines, data generators, and debugging utilities—that dramatically reduce the friction of retro development.

It acts less like a tool and more like an endlessly patient, optimistic collaborator who helps bridge knowledge gaps and unlock creative flow.

This talk explores practical examples, unexpected strengths (and limitations), and what it means to pair human intuition with AI when building games—and the tools around them—across 40 years of technology.
sessions.title: Retro Meets AI: Shipping Games Across 40 Years of Tech

sessions.intendedAudience: Ledere, arkitekter og utviklere som jobber i virksomheter med teknologi man helst skulle erstattet
sessions.abstract:
Virksomheter i dag er begrenset av gamle IT-løsninger. Ofte har man forsøkt å erstatte gamle systemer flere ganger uten hell. For å lykkes har vi tatt i kombinert forståelse av organisasjon og ledelse med innsikt i teknologi og arbeidsprosesser. I dette foredraget forteller vi hvordan lederen som forstår teknologi og folk jobber sammen med teknologen som brenner for organisasjonens mål.

Politiet har erfaring med å forsøke å fornye gamle, samfunnskritiske systemer. Vi har erfart at den klassiske prosjektmodellen er en dårlig match for viktige softwareprosjekter. Utredninger og forprosjekt koster mye, men hjelper i liten grad å avdekke reel risiko. I samfunnskritiske systemer kan smidige prosesser høres utrygt ut, men vår erfaring er at smidig-skeptikere blir til fans når vi klarer å levere fungerende programvare som brukerne skryter av.

I foredraget får du høre både hvordan man navigerer i byråkratiet, organiserer team og skaffer handlingsrom, samtidig som vi snakker om konkrete teknikker for å forstå systemene og arbeidsprosessene, utvikle nye løsninger som kan tas i bruk underveis og reduserer risikoen for brukere som er skuffet over den nye funksjonaliteten eller (mangelen på) stabiliteten i løsningen
sessions.title: Å skifte vinger i lufta eller hjul mens sykkelen er i nedoverbakke: Modernisering av samfunnskritiske systemer uten høye skuldre

sessions.intendedAudience: Java developers working on data-intensive or event-driven systems. Data engineers and platform engineers. Solution architects evaluating modern data platform patterns. Teams exploring AI-ready data architectures without vendor lock-in.
sessions.abstract:
Modern AI and analytics workloads punish architectures built on copying data between systems. In this workshop, we build a vendor-neutral, open-source HTAP stack that combines transactional, analytical, and AI-oriented workloads without the usual ETL tax. The architecture is based on a practical demo stack using Apache Cassandra for operational data, Apache Kafka for ingestion, Apache Spark for large-scale processing, Presto for interactive SQL, and open table and search technologies such as Parquet, Iceberg, and OpenSearch.

Participants will bring up the stack locally, stream events into it, query live operational data, run analytical queries, and export data for downstream use. Along the way, we will discuss why real-time context, cost control, and reducing duplication matter for modern systems, especially when building AI-ready platforms and agent-facing applications. 

This is not a product pitch. It is a hands-on architecture workshop for developers and architects who want to understand how open-source building blocks fit together to support low-latency apps, analytics, and AI use cases in one platform.
sessions.title: From Streams to SQL to AI. Build an Open Source HTAP Platform in 4 Hours
sessions.workshopPrerequisites:
Participants should prepare the following before the workshop:

1. A laptop with Docker Compose or Podman Compose installed.
2. At least 12 GB RAM available for the local environment for bringing up the full stack locally
3. Git installed
4. A terminal and an editor or IDE of choice
5. Optional but useful: Java and basic command-line comfort

Before the session, participants should clone the workshop repository and verify they can start containers locally.

sessions.intendedAudience: Java developers
sessions.abstract:
This presentation introduces Jenesis, a proof of concept build tool for Java that explores a new approach to lowering the language's entry barrier. Rather than relying on external installation or non Java configuration, Jenesis can be embedded directly as source code and executed and modified like any other Java program. This enables a more approachable and transparent build experience for both newcomers and seasoned developers.

A second focus is security. By rethinking how external dependencies are introduced and managed, Jenesis seeks to reduce software supply chain risks while maintaining reproducible builds. The tool also separates build logic from dependency declaration and leverages the Java module system to promote clearer project boundaries and improved maintainability.
sessions.title: Do We Need a Build Language? Java Builds Java

sessions.intendedAudience: This talk is for developers, data scientists, data engineers, technical architects, and anyone interested in building decision systems from real operational data. The audience will see two concrete examples of how business problems can be modeled as graph optimization and routing problems, and how to make those systems explainable, testable, and production-ready. No prior background in optimization or quantum computing is required, but some experience with software systems, data pipelines, or backend engineering will help.
sessions.abstract:
Public transport is full of decisions that sound simple until you try to automate them. Where should self-driving vehicles wait between trips? Which fare-inspection tasks should teams execute today, in which order, under real operational constraints?
In this talk, we show how we built two optimization systems for public transit at Ruter. The first turns historical demand into a weighted graph and computes where vehicles should be staged, using production-ready exact solvers and quantum solvers as an emerging alternative. The second turns stop-level inspection risk into executable day plans using routing with time windows, onboard service times, and a tunable balance between efficiency, coverage, and unpredictability.
This is a practical engineering talk about choosing the right problem model, validating solver behavior, and building pipelines that operators can trust. Attendees will leave with concrete patterns for turning optimization from a notebook experiment into a production system.
sessions.title: How We Built Public-Transport Optimization Systems with Classical and Quantum Solvers
sessions.suggestedKeywords: optimization, operations research, routing, public transport, self-driving, autonomous vehicles, quantum computing, decision systems

Alongside this research background, she works on optimization and operational decision systems in public transport at Ruter, with projects including vehicle staging for demand-responsive transport and fare-inspection planning under real operational constraints. Her work combines mathematical modeling, graph and routing methods, classical optimization, and exploratory quantum approaches.

sessions.intendedAudience: Java Developers in general might benefit from understanding how the JVM works
sessions.abstract:
We all use Java everywhere, from IoT devices and desktops to servers and even mainframes.
All we need these days is made available in frameworks and libraries but how much do we know about what’s going on in the Java Virtual Machine.
This session will try to give you an idea of how Java source code will be transformed from text to byte code in classes, to be interpreted and later eventually become compiled machine code.
How is code loaded into the JVM, which of this code will be interpreted or compiled and why? Why will some code be optimised and others not? How does the JVM handle the memory management? What are the good and bad parts of JIT compilation? How does the startup of the JVM work? 
If you are interested in the answers to these and more questions, this session will be for you.
sessions.title: A Journey - On tour with Java code on it’s way through the JVM

sessions.intendedAudience: Developers
sessions.abstract:
Have you ever worked on a project where the history of the system vanished? Ten years have passed, no one from the original team works there anymore, and no one understands what the data even does anymore. We have all been there. Usually we store the current state, but that looses information about the journey - all that valuable context about what happened over time.

What if we could change it? What if you didn't just have the current state of the application, but also how it got there!
This might sound like magic, but it is possible through Event Sourcing! In this workshop, we introduce concepts around Event Sourcing and how it can be used to orchestrate an online store. We want to show how you define events, create and listen to event-streams, and how you can build database models through events.

This workshop is on an introductory level and fits well for developers who want to learn basic Event Sourcing techniques.
sessions.title: Event Sourcing: From Chaos to Control

sessions.intendedAudience: DevOps Engineers, Linux users, developers
sessions.abstract:
As developers, we version-control our code, containerize our services, and declare our infrastructure as code. But when it comes to our own workstations? We're still manually installing packages, debugging broken updates, and praying that a kernel upgrade doesn't tank our day before a deadline.

Immutable Linux distributions flip this on its head. Your operating system becomes a versioned, reproducible artifact — built from a Containerfile or a declarative config file, atomically updated, and trivially rolled back when things go wrong.

In this talk, we'll explore two approaches reshaping how developers run Linux. First, Fedora Atomic and Universal Blue, where your OS is literally an OCI container image — built with the same Dockerfiles and CI/CD pipelines you already know, shipped through container registries, and deployed with `bootc`. If you can write a Dockerfile, you can build an operating system. Second, NixOS, where a single configuration file defines your entire system — packages, services, firewall rules, everything — and `nixos-rebuild switch` atomically applies it. Hand a colleague your config and they get your exact environment. No more "works on my machine."

We'll demo atomic rollbacks, disposable dev environments with Toolbx and `nix develop`, and how Universal Blue's Bluefin ships a developer mode with VS Code, Podman, and Kubernetes tooling baked into the OS image. We'll also look at how `nix develop` can replace your project's README setup instructions with a single command that gives every contributor an identical development shell.

Whether you're tired of polluting your base system with project dependencies, managing a fleet of developer workstations, or just want an OS that treats reproducibility as a first-class citizen, this talk will change how you think about your desktop.
sessions.title: Immutable Linux - The Future of the desktop

sessions.intendedAudience: anyone concerned about personal digital security
sessions.abstract:
Random number generators (RNGs) generate "supposedly" random numbers for use in your application or solution.  However, most RNGs are algorithmic, "pseudo", that anyone who deeply understands the algorithm may be able to regenerate the "random" numbers.  Often this isn't too concerning: go ahead, recreate the shuffled songs played by Sonos.

But when it's important, it's REALLY important: cryptographic keys, digital signatures, authentication challenges, statistical analysis, gaming and gambling.  Different algorithms have different entropy: higher entropy leading to better randomness - assuming, of course, no back doors were injected!  That said, even strong algorithms displaying high entropy _may_ have faults to exploit.  Should you be concerned?

In this session we'll dig into RNGs, pseduo-RNGs and, for the truly concerned or paranoid, learn to create custom true RNGs that are impossible to hack.  Hope you can join us!
sessions.title: Personalizing Your Random Numbers

sessions.intendedAudience: All Team Members, Testers, Developers, Team Leaders
sessions.abstract:
Elevator Pitch
---
In the globalized tech landscape, understanding cultural influences on testing practices has never been more critical. I have conducted a study with contributions from testing professionals across 20+ countries and 15+ nationalities, revealing how deeply cultural values impact work styles.

Study website: https://mesutdurukal.github.io/Global-Testing-Practices/ 

Problem Statement
---
Global software teams frequently encounter challenges stemming from unaddressed cultural differences rather than technical deficiencies. Teams struggle with misaligned expectations and inefficient communication patterns that arise when working across different cultural contexts. I’ve had experiences where I realized I was communicating with people in the wrong way, which led to failures even though I had enough context.

Another aspect is, there's often resistance to process changes and inconsistent quality standards as different regions prioritize various aspects of testing differently. Additionally, conflicting approaches to risk and innovation can create friction in distributed teams, leading to suboptimal testing outcomes and decreased team effectiveness. 

Solutions
---
This study provides a comprehensive framework for understanding and addressing cultural influences on testing practices. The talk offers practical strategies for bridging cultural gaps in distributed teams, focusing on creating shared understanding and effective collaboration. The session includes real-world approaches to harmonize testing practices across different cultural contexts, supported by case studies of successful multicultural testing teams. Attendees will gain actionable insights to improve their global testing strategies and foster better cross-cultural collaboration.

Key Takeaways
---
1. Cultural Archetypes in Testing: How hierarchical vs. individualistic cultures approach quality
2. Communication Patterns: Direct vs. indirect feedback in test reviews
3. Risk Appetite: How different cultures balance speed vs. quality
4. Automation Adoption: Cultural barriers and accelerators
5. Leadership Styles: What works where (and why)

Why This Talk Matters
---
While most testing conferences focus on tools and methodologies, this session addresses the human factors that ultimately determine their success. The unique, data-driven approach combines global research with real-world experiences from global testing professionals.

Summary
---
This session will transform how you think about testing in a global context, providing actionable insights to improve collaboration, quality, and efficiency in culturally diverse teams. You'll leave with practical strategies to navigate cultural complexities and build more effective testing practices. The diverse participant pool includes contributors from Japan, Palestine, Thailand, India, Netherlands, Sweden, Russia, Hungary, USA, Philippines, UAE, Germany, and more, providing a truly global perspective on testing practices.
sessions.title: Global Cultural Reflections on Software Testing Practices
sessions.suggestedKeywords: Cultural Intelligence, Global Testing, Distributed Teams, Quality Engineering, Agile Testing, Cross-cultural Communication, Leadership in Testing, Quality Culture, International Software Development

sessions.intendedAudience: Developers, Architects
sessions.abstract:
In an agentic world, software no longer just responds to user input — it acts on our behalf. Decisions are made by AI agents, workflows are executed autonomously, and systems continuously change themselves. When something goes wrong, “the state” is no longer a sufficient answer.

This talk explores why event sourcing becomes foundational in AI-driven systems. Event types don’t just record what changed, but capture why it changed, who initiated it, on whose behalf, and what caused it — including the originating prompt, model, and decision chain.

By preserving identity, intent, causation, and context as first-class data, event-sourced systems provide something traditional architectures cannot: a complete, trustworthy audit trail that survives autonomous execution. Not logs. Not snapshots. A factual history.

You’ll see how causation chains, correlated events, and rich metadata turn production systems into explainable systems — enabling accountability, debugging, governance, and trust when humans are no longer in the loop for every action.

If AI agents are going to act for us, we need architectures that can answer for them.
sessions.title: Event Sourcing: The Only Sane Way to Run Agentic Systems

sessions.intendedAudience: Alle som jobber med produktutvikling, både teammedlemmer og ledere.
sessions.abstract:
Fra høsten 2025 til høsten 2026 følger vi 15 team fra 8 organisasjoner for å forstå hva som faktisk skjer når AI-agenter tas i bruk i praksis, ikke bare hva vi tror skal skje. Det gir oss et unikt innblikk i hvordan ulike team nærmer seg AI og hvorfor noen kommer videre enn andre.  

I tillegg samlet vi i juni 2026 over 30 av norges ledende utviklere, tech leads og forskere til åpne samtaler om det vi ser og det vi ennå ikke forstår. Vi stilte spørsmål som: Hvordan ser fremtidens programvareutvikling ut som sosioteknisk system, med vår arbeidskultur, våre regulatoriske rammer og vår tradisjon for autonome team? Når utviklingstakten øker og mer arbeid automatiseres, blir lærende team enda viktigere - men hvordan skaper vi rom for det i praksis? Hvilke ferdigheter trenger fremtidens teammedlemmer, og hvem er de? Hva skjer med rekruttering og læring når mange av de tradisjonelle junioroppgavene forsvinner? Og hvordan endrer AI måten vi designer systemer, skriver kode og organiserer utviklingsarbeidet på? 

I forskningsprosjektet KAIROS følger vi norske utviklingsteam over fire år. Dette er et samarbeid mellom Knowit, TV2, Storebrand, Politiet, Avinor, SINTEF Digital og Norges forskningsråd. Vi studerer hvordan AI-verktøy endrer samarbeid, koordinering, roller og arbeidsprosesser i norske utviklingsteam.
sessions.title: Norsk forskning: Hva gjør norske organisasjoner med agentisk utvikling

speakers.name: Jan Henrik Gundelsby
speakers.bio: Jan Henrik har over 25 års erfaring som utvikler, arkitekt og teknologileder i store prosjekter og programmer. Han har jobbet med store strategiske satsninger på digitalt førstevalg og digital transformasjon, og har vært sentral i å bygge DevOps-kultur, mikrotjenestearkitektur og produktorganisasjon hos store virksomheter. Jan Henrik var tidligere CTO i Knowit, og er nå leder for Knowit Research. Der jobber han med å tette gapet mellom akademia og praksis, med fokus på hvordan AI endrer samarbeid, roller og arbeidsprosesser i utviklingsteam. Han er også forsker i KAIROS-prosjektet, der han sammen med SINTEF Digital studerer norske utviklingsteam som tar i bruk AI-agenter.

speakers.name: Nils Brede Moe
speakers.bio: Nils Brede Moe er sjefforsker ved SINTEF Digital — alltid med godt humør. Han forsker på hvordan team samarbeider, koordinerer og tar beslutninger, med særlig fokus på hvordan AI-verktøy endrer arbeidsprosesser i utviklingsteam. Han leder forskningen i KAIROS-prosjektet, der han følger norske utviklingsteam som tar i bruk AI-agenter. Nils Brede jobber tett med store virksomheter innen energi, telekom, transport og finans, i tillegg til konsulentselskaper og offentlig sektor i Norge. Han har en forskningsstilling ved Blekinge Institute of Technology i Sverige og er fast spaltist i E24.

sessions.intendedAudience: Developer, Architect
sessions.abstract: As AI rapidly evolves from model experimentation to production-grade applications, Java developers are increasingly faced with a new paradigm: designing intelligent systems that can reason, interact, and adapt. This talk explores the emerging Java AI application development journey through the lens of agent-based architecture, with a focus on how the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and Agent2Agent (A2A) communication patterns are transforming the way we build intelligent, context-aware services. We’ll walk through the foundational shifts in building AI-native Java applications—moving beyond single-shot prompt-response interactions to multiagent coordination, persistent memory, and tool-augmented reasoning. We’ll also address the critical architectural trade-offs: performance versus flexibility, open source versus vendor-managed inference, local versus remote model deployment, and how to maintain security and observability in agent-based systems.
sessions.title: Building Intelligent Java Apps: Agent Patterns, MCP, and the Future of AI Native Design
sessions.suggestedKeywords: Java, Agent, MCP, A2A, Quarkus, Java AI

speakers.name: Daniel Oh
speakers.bio: Java Champion, CNCF Ambassador & TAG DevEX Co-Chair, Microsoft MVP, Developer Advocate, Technical Marketing, Keynote Speaker, Published Author

sessions.intendedAudience: Everyone can attend this session since I'll start from scratch/beginner level, but it will be extra interesting backend developers. The participants will gain a short and pain-free introduction to Rust and how it can be easily used for side projects.
sessions.abstract:
On October 1st 2025, Open AI announces buying 900 000 wafers DRAM, about 40% of the global market production. As a direct consequence, some DDR5 kits that cost about 300€ in 2023, exceeded 1.000€ by the beginning of 2026. The SSD market was also impacted  with price"only" doubling so far. How long will it take "non-AI" data centers to run out of stock and start raising their prices to compensate for future hardware investment?

On the developer side, we have a plethora of backend frameworks based on the V8 JS engine, the JVM and similar technologies. They all have one thing in common: CPU and memory consumption. But most cloud providers charge by how much you use those resources. We can clearly guess the direction of our cloud service invoices: it will go up! 

What if we had a language slashing your costs by 90%? Enters Rust, the most-admired programming language in 2025.

Join me in this lightning talk to discover the world of backend services written with Rust. We'll start from scratch,  by going through the tools we need to get started, the different backend framework available and how we can deploy our first backend API.
sessions.title: Rust will slash your backend costs

speakers.name: Jean Tuffier
speakers.bio: Hi! I'm the team lead for the "Onboard" team at Entur. We deliver an app used by thousands employees of public transport companies in Norway. I have more than 10 years of professional experience with Android development. I also have some iOS, web and Rust projects on the side. You can reach me at https://mastodon.social/@tufekoi

sessions.intendedAudience: Dette foredraget er relevant for alle som er interesserte i hacking-kulturen, og som vil vite hvor lett man kunne hacke ting i gode, gamle dager!
sessions.abstract:
For noen tiår siden var mulighetene for hacking helt annerledes enn i dag. Samfunnet var ikke like gjennomdigitalisert, og sikkerhet var rett og slett ikke noe som ble prioritert.

I dag har både nasjonalstater og kriminelle organisasjoner høy aktivitet innen datakriminalitet, men litt tilbake i tid var det gjerne ungdommer som utforsket hva som gikk an å få til.

Foredraget har derfor et bredt spenn, og vi kan nevne "komme-seg-inn-der-vi-ikke-har-lov" både digitalt og fysisk, utnyttelse av telefonsystemer, manipulering av meningsmålinger, demoscenen på Amiga, det famøse copypartyet i Drammen som ble raidet av politiet, og mye annet.

Dette er et foredrag som gjør at du både vil trekke på smilebåndene og få en en unik innsikt i hvordan det var i "gamle" dager.
sessions.title: Hacking i "gamle" dager - røverhistorier fra 80- og 90-tallet

speakers.name: Eivind Vea
speakers.bio: Eivind er utdannet sivilingeniør fra NTH/NTNU hvor han studerte på 90-tallet. Han brukte utallige netter på datasalene der han tweaket teknologi, lover og regler i det som han kaller "kreativ bruk av teknologi". Han har jobbet som konsulent i Bouvet siden 2019.

sessions.intendedAudience: Everyone!
sessions.abstract:
AI har gjort det billigere å skrive kode, skissere løsninger og komme i gang. Men det har ikke nødvendigvis gjort det lettere å skape flyt.

Mange team opplever det samme: tempoet går opp, men også støyen. Flere ideer, flere prototyper, flere forslag, flere avbrytelser. Når AI kan hjelpe oss med nesten alt, blir det plutselig enda viktigere å forstå hva som faktisk skaper fremdrift.

I dette foredraget ser vi på hvordan “flyt” endrer seg i en AI-preget arbeidshverdag. Ikke bare personlig flyt for utvikleren, men flyt i teamet, i beslutningene og i verdistrømmen. Hva skjer med kvalitet, eierskap og fokus når en stor del av det operative arbeidet blir lettere? Og hvordan bygger vi arbeidsformer som gjør at AI forsterker oss, i stedet for å fragmentere oppmerksomheten vår?

Dette er et foredrag for utviklere, tech leads og ledere som vil bruke AI til mer enn å produsere mer kode: de vil skape bedre flyt.
sessions.title: Flyt i AI-ens tid: når det blir lettere å lage, men vanskeligere å bevege seg

speakers.bio: Lead Software Engineer at Telenor | Professional Kanban Trainer at ProKanban.org

sessions.suggestedKeywords: Design, mentale modeller, nyutvikling, utfasing, tidsmaskin

speakers.bio: Jeg er produktdesigner i politiets IT-enhet og jobber vanligvis med digitale anmeldelser.  Jeg brenner for gode brukeropplevelser, og for å få til det tenker jeg at vi trenger utviklere som er nysgjerrige på brukeren, og designere som er nysgjerrige på det teknologiske mulighetsrommet. sessions.intendedAudience: Backend developers, architects, and security engineers working with Java in enterprise or regulated environments.
sessions.abstract:
Quantum computing is no longer a distant concept—it’s an imminent challenge to today’s cryptographic standards. The shift to Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) is not optional; it’s inevitable. Yet, the practical steps for adapting existing applications to PQC, and the critical considerations for ensuring compatibility, remain largely unclear. This session goes beyond theory. We’ll start with a concise overview of PQC fundamentals, then dive into real-world migration experiences—what worked, what didn’t, and why. Attendees will gain actionable best practices drawn from hands-on projects, offering guidance not only for those planning future migrations but also for developers designing new applications today. If you’ve ever wondered, “Where do I start?” or “What pitfalls should I avoid?”, this talk will provide the answers—grounded in real-world experience and focused on practical success.

By the end of this session, participants will be able to:
- Understand the fundamentals of Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) and why early adoption is critical in the face of quantum computing threats.
- Identify key challenges and pitfalls encountered during real-world PQC migration projects.
- Apply proven best practices for adapting existing applications to PQC while maintaining security and performance.
- Develop a practical roadmap for integrating PQC into both legacy systems and new application designs.
sessions.title: Quantum-Ready Java: A Practical Guide to Post-Quantum Cryptography Migration
sessions.suggestedKeywords: Security, Cryptography, Post-Quantum, PQC, Migration, JDK, NIST

speakers.name: Akihiro Nishikawa
speakers.bio:
Aki, working for Microsoft as a Cloud Solution Architect, is passionate about A JVM/GraalVM and JVM-based open-source technologies. He is also a board member of JJUG (Japan Java Users Group). He leverages his expertise in Java, cloud-native architecture, application integration, observability, and security to assist enterprise customers in designing and modernizing Java-based systems. In this way, he bridges the gap between platform innovation and the realities of production environments.
He has also spoken at tech conferences in Japan and abroad, including JavaZone, Jfokus, JavaLand, Devoxx France, and JNation.

sessions.intendedAudience: De som jobber med teknologien, men også ledere og de som må sette ned regler og lover rundt dette
sessions.abstract:
KI brukes eller forsøkes til absolutt alt nå. Derimot finnes det mange iboende problemer med KI som knapt snakkes om, trolig fordi konsekvensene tar årevis for å bli synlige. Selv uten dommedagsscenarioene som preger mediedekningen kan kostnadene kan bli enorme uansett. KI krever din respekt.
Vi er nå på toppen av hype-bølgen, og vil snart begynne å se hva KI faktisk kan brukes til. Dot-com, agile, big data, blockchain, low-code: bransjen vår har en lang historikk av hype-sykluser, og hvor man i etterkant fant ut hvor teknologien egentlig hørte hjemme.
KI skiller seg likevel ut fra tidligere teknologiske skift, både i potensialet for å forbedre livene våre og i omfanget av skaden den kan gjøre. Kom og få innsikt i de langsiktige kostnadene som sjeldent diskuteres utenfor akademia. Lær hvilke risikoer du bør ha i bakhodet når du vurderer å bruke, designe eller integrere KI i prosjektene dine. Se hvordan valgene vi tar de neste årene kan få økende konsekvenser over de neste titalls årene, for oss og for våre barn.
Ansvarlig bruk av KI forutsetter oversikt over alle kostnader og risikoer, inkludert de vi slipper å bære selv. Først da kan vi ta informerte beslutninger og virkelig høste verdi fra KI.
sessions.title: De usette langtidskostnadene med dagens KI

speakers.name: Magnus Elden
speakers.bio: Magnus Elden er en erfaren speaker med lang erfaring som både utvikler og arkitekt. Han er en aktiv konsulent som jobber for private og offentlige aktører, små og store prosjekter, med design, coaching, rådføring, og mer. Frivillig styremedlem for OSWA.

sessions.intendedAudience: React developers
sessions.abstract:
Building app-like UX on the web has historically meant reaching for an SPA. You ship the data layer to the browser, manage a client cache, juggle loading states, coordinate mutations, and write a lot of code to keep the UI feeling fast and fresh. The result can be great, but the cost is high, and the model gets harder to maintain as your app grows.

Next.js takes a different path. Since React Server Components first landed, we’ve been working toward a way to build where each piece of your app runs where it belongs, while the mental model stays the same: components. In this talk, you’ll see how that translates into instant-feeling UX, streamed UI, fresh data, coordinated updates, caching across the stack, and strong Core Web Vitals that hold up as your product scales.
sessions.title: What RSCs can do in Next.js today
sessions.suggestedKeywords: react, nextjs, react server components, performance, ecommerce

speakers.name: Aurora Scharff
speakers.bio: Aurora Scharff is a DX Engineer on the Next.js team at Vercel and the React Certification Lead at certificates.dev, focused on technical content, developer education, and community. She contributes to the Next.js framework, builds developer tooling, creates demo applications, writes deep-dive blog posts, and delivers workshops and talks at conferences worldwide.

sessions.intendedAudience: Java developers (and data engineers) who want to work with Apache Parquet, but without the Hadoop baggage
sessions.abstract:
Apache Parquet has become the lingua franca of the modern data ecosystem, powering data lakes and table formats like Apache Iceberg—but for Java, the go-to library parquet-java pulls in Hadoop and a truckload of other dependencies, and its reader is single-threaded. This was bugging me enough to start Hardwood, a brand-new Parquet parser written from scratch in modern Java, applying some of the performance lessons learned from the One Billion Row Challenge. Come and join me for this talk, where we'll look at:

- The internals of the Parquet format and what makes parallelizing its decoding surprisingly tricky
- Techniques for achieving high throughput, such as page-level parallelism, adaptive prefetching, and avoiding auto-boxing in hot loops
- How to use JDK Flight Recorder for identifying performance bottlenecks
- Practical learnings from using AI (specifically, Claude Code) as a coding companion—what works well, where you need to stay sharp, and why "built with AI" doesn't mean "vibe-coded"

Whether you're interested in file formats, Java performance, or getting a realistic take on AI-assisted development, there should be something in here for you.
sessions.title: Hardwood: Building a Parquet Parser From Scratch (With a Little Help From AI)
sessions.suggestedKeywords: apache parquet, performance, parsing, big data, AI

speakers.name: Gunnar Morling
speakers.bio: Gunnar Morling is an open-source software engineer in the Java and data streaming space, currently working as a Technologist at Confluent. Previously, he helped to build a realtime stream processing platform based on Apache Flink and led the Debezium project, a distributed platform for change data capture. He is a Java Champion and has founded multiple open source projects such as Hardwood, kcctl, JfrUnit, and MapStruct. Gunnar is an avid blogger (morling.dev) and has spoken at various conferences like QCon, Java One, and Devoxx. He lives in Hamburg, Germany.

sessions.intendedAudience: Cassandra enthusiasts, Java performance enthusiasts, Intermediate/Senior Java eng
sessions.abstract:
The Cassandra database is a widely deployed Java infrastructure piece, and a critical part of many services. A core part of it's work is deduplicating and deleting data through periodic compactions. In this talk I will discuss a recent patch in which I re-implemented compaction to deliver a large performance improvement. This is an exploration of the delivery of a meaningful performance improvement to a long standing, industry standard, OSS project.

Key Takeaways:
- Picking the right target
- Setting meaningful goals and metrics
- The contribution process
- Re-occurring optimisation techniques
sessions.title: Cassandra Compaction - Allocation Free, and 5x Faster

speakers.name: Nitsan Wakart
speakers.bio: A blogger and a coder with a pedantic passion for performance. My work has spanned army intelligence systems, dot.com era startups, financial institutions and innovative product companies. A JVM Engineer for Apple by day, main contributor to lock free data structures library JCTools by night.

sessions.intendedAudience: Utviklere og arkitekter som har jobbet med EHF, fakturering eller integrasjoner mot offentlig sektor og er nysgjerrige på hva som egentlig ligger under. Ingen forkunnskaper om Peppol eller EU-regulering nødvendig.
sessions.abstract:
Du sender en EHF-faktura. Den går gjennom en aksesspunkt-leverandør du ikke kjenner, via et nettverk du ikke kontrollerer, til en mottaker du ikke har noen direkte relasjon til. Likevel forventer du at den kommer frem, og at du kan stole på svaret.

Det nettverket heter Peppol. Det har båret EHF-fakturaen i over ti år, og store deler av norsk næringsliv er allerede tilkoblet uten å vite om det. Peppol er ikke et selskap eller en plattform. Det er et nettverk der nasjonale myndigheter og over 700 sertifiserte tjenesteleverandører på tvers av Europa, Asia og Australia har blitt enige om å stole på hverandre. Ingen sitter i midten. Ingen har full kontroll.

Hvordan er det mulig?
I dette foredraget tar vi deg inn i infrastrukturen bak fakturaen, og inn i det vi nå jobber med: neste generasjon tillitsarkitektur for nettverket. Hva er det egentlig som gjør at meldingen finner frem? Hvor holder et sertifikathierarki opp med å være tilstrekkelig svar? Og hva skjer med tillitstopologien når du distribuerer en tjeneste som ingen eide alene fra starten av?

Underveis ser vi også fremover: EUs kommende European Business Wallet vil endre hvordan virksomheter identifiserer seg digitalt – og det vil påvirke nettverk som Peppol og systemene som er koblet til dem.

Tillit er ikke bare et teknisk problem. Det er et arkitekturproblem. Og det starter med en EHF-faktura du trodde du kjente.
sessions.title: EHF-fakturaen din har reist verden rundt – men visste du det?
sessions.suggestedKeywords: EHF, Peppol, trust architecture, distributed systems, European Business Wallet

speakers.name: Rune Kjørlaug
speakers.bio: Rune Kjørlaug er senior enterprise-arkitekt og medeier i Emning AS. Han jobber med tillits- og sikkerhetsarkitektur for Peppol-nettverket, og har bakgrunn fra Brønnøysundregistrene, DFØ og Difi. Han har jobbet med digital infrastruktur og identitetsarkitektur i over 15 år, og brenner for systemer der governance og teknologi må snakke samme språk.

speakers.name: Charlotte Kolle
speakers.bio: Charlotte Kolle er medeier i Emning AS og prosjektleder for Peppols Critical Infrastructure Working Group 2.0. Hun er spesialist på offentlige anskaffelser med bred erfaring fra domenet, og bringer et solid brukerperspektiv på hva tillitsinfrastruktur faktisk må løse for virksomheter som kjøper og selger i offentlig sektor.

sessions.intendedAudience: developers and others interested in cryptographic proofs
sessions.abstract:
Have you ever wanted to prove you meet an age requirement without revealing any information about your identity? That's exactly what you need to do when a website that respects anonymity needs to be sure it offers services for the right age group. This is just one application of a concept known as zero-knowledge proof.

Zero-knowledge proof is simply a concept where you prove you know something -- or more generally that some statement is true -- without revealing anything about what you know -- or about that statement.

In this lightning talk, we will examine how to prove you know something without actually spilling the beans about what you actually know.
sessions.title: Knowing without Knowing

speakers.name: Jørn K Baltzersen
speakers.bio: Jorn K. Baltzersen is an experienced developer through more than a couple of decades. He holds an M.Sc. in engineering and computer science from the previous century and an M.Sc. (summa cum laude) in blockchain and digital currency (2023) from the University of Nicosia. He is a Principal Solution Consultant at Tieto Tech Consulting.

sessions.intendedAudience: The topic is fairly advanced. The hope is that with an introduction, and demonstration of LLM capabilities, it will be more accessible to all experience levels.
sessions.abstract:
The JVM enables rich introspection with advanced tools such as JMX, flight recorder, mission control and all the goodies in the bin folder of your JDK.

These are however, expert tools, and may have a quite high entry threshold. Where do you begin, and what strategies should you use to troubleshoot your particular problem?

LLMs are now the solution when you need to get started on a new skill in unfamiliar territory.

We will look at ongoing MCP initiatives to bridge the gap between the information present in the JVM and your troubleshooting effort.

This way you can express the problem you are trying to solve, without knowing every in and out of these advanced tools and troubleshooting strategies.

The presentation will partly be based on live demo. Therefore, with LLMs in the mix, I am as curious on the outcome as you are!
Bring a pinch of salt.
sessions.title: Talk to me Java!

speakers.name: Martin Skarsaune
speakers.bio: Martin is a Developer and Co-Owner at Kantega. He has struggled to understand the JVM ever since the last millenium. This has led him down a path to join several monitoring projects such as JDK Mission Control,  Jolokia and hawt.io.

sessions.intendedAudience: Java and Spring developers
sessions.abstract: Spring Boot 4.x and Java 25+ have arrived, making it an exciting time to be a Java developer! Join me, Josh Long (@starbuxman), as we dive into the future of Spring Boot with Java 25. We'll learn how to scale your applications and codebases effortlessly, explore the robust Spring Boot ecosystem featuring AI, modularity, seamless data access, security, and cutting-edge production optimizations like Project Loom's virtual threads, GraalVM, AppCDS, and more. Let's explore the latest-and-greatest in Spring Boot to build faster, more scalable, more efficient, more modular, more secure, and more intelligent systems and services.

speakers.name: Josh Long

sessions.intendedAudience: Developers
sessions.abstract:
Come & Code ALL Together a Game Master (GM) that will be responsible for orchestrating multiple AI agents, each specialized in a particular task.

We'll explore concepts like agent tools, the MCP protocol (Model Context Protocol), A2A (Agent to Agent), and RAG (Retrieval-augmented generation) to create agents and/or MCP servers that roll dice, verify DnD rules, and generate the story as it unfolds.

Whether you're a role-playing game fan or not, there will be things to learn during this session :)

P.S: Here the content I developed for my submission => https://github.com/aws-samples/sample-once-upon-spring-ai
sessions.title: Create Your Own Role-Playing Game with Agentic AI using Spring AI (Delivered as a Workshop)

speakers.name: Arnaud Jean
speakers.bio:
Helping Devs build cool stuff, learn faster, and laugh along the way — one bug at a time.

𝘈𝘭𝘭 𝘷𝘪𝘦𝘸𝘴 𝘦𝘹𝘱𝘳𝘦𝘴𝘴𝘦𝘥 𝘢𝘳𝘦 𝘮𝘺 𝘰𝘸𝘯.

sessions.intendedAudience: Everyone. Participants will be better equipped to discuss non-idiot questions about AI.
sessions.abstract: The AI hype wave is washing over us, fueled by advances in generative AI tools. It's a mixture of grandiose claims, speculations, euphoria and panic. Everyone wants to demonstrate that they are on board the AI train choo-chooing towards the future, that they're not getting left behind. The rhetoric will have us act, not think - there is no time! Is that true? It's a paradox that we are supposed to meet artificial intelligence with our own intelligence turned off. If it's true that AI will "change everything", surely we need every ounce of human intelligence - as well as compassion, wisdom and humanity - we can muster! And what if it turns out to be false? At what cost do we participate in this dance?
sessions.title: The AI puppet dance

speakers.name: Einar Waaler Høst
speakers.bio: Einar W. Høst is a software developer at Norkart. He enjoys collaborative modelling, API design and computer programming. He has a PhD in Computer Science from the University of Oslo.

sessions.intendedAudience: People interested in learning the fundamentals of common LLM APIs
sessions.abstract:
When you want to integrate AI into your tools, what do you do? You add langchain4j or spring-ai and run with it. These fairly big libraries work well. But do you know how these libraries interact with the AI service providers or your local LLM instance? These libraries aren't magic; they are just abstractions of the underlying REST APIs.

In this talk, we'll peek behind the curtain, write a tiny LLM library together live on stage, and use it to build a tiny chatbot with tool support.
sessions.title: Let’s create a tiny LLM library together

speakers.name: Johannes Bechberger
speakers.bio: Johannes Bechberger is a JVM developer working on profilers and their underlying technology in the SapMachine team at SAP. This includes improvements to async-profiler and its ecosystem, a website to view the different JFR event types, and improvements to the FirefoxProfiler, making it usable in the Java world. His work today comprises many open-source contributions and his blog, where he regularly writes on in-depth profiling and debugging topics. He also works on hello-ebpf, the first eBPF library for Java. His most recent contribution is the new CPU Time Profiler in JDK 25.

sessions.intendedAudience: Any developer interested in AI (that is, everybody). It's helpful as we all work to figure out AI's impact on our profession to know the process by which it has come to us.
sessions.abstract:
Up until early 2023, I regularly said AI would always have a bright future—and I didn't mean that as a compliment. Sure, deep learning had made us good at building very impressive classifiers in the decade or so prior, but for so long, human-like intelligence was just five years in the future—and that made me a skeptic. Things are different now, but how much skepticism is still warranted? What is it that we've got on our hands? What changes is modern AI bringing with it? Like with so many other questions, the answers are easier if we understand where we've come from.

Beginning with the Turing Test itself, the famous Dartmouth Conference of 1956, and the Perceptron of 1957, we'll trace decades of disappointment and broken promises as we tried to realize the true potential of computing, at the same time grasping for an understanding of what it means to be human. Taking a close look at various technologies along the way, we'll arrive in the 21st century, the revival of neural networks, the advent of the Transformer, and a revolutionary new technology category that has prompted oracles of weal and woe from our most optimistic and apocalyptic technology prophets.

Standing on the edge of the unknown, what elements of the 75-year-old promise have we realized? A close examination gives us a better view of our immediate future as technologists and insight into what it means to be human.
sessions.title: A Brief History of Artificial Intelligence
sessions.suggestedKeywords: AI, ML, Deep Learning, LLM, Neural Network

sessions.intendedAudience: Developers and platform engineers working with Kubernetes or cloud infrastructure who want to simplify provisioning through self-service platforms. Basic familiarity with Kubernetes and cloud concepts is beneficial, but no prior Crossplane experience is required.
sessions.abstract:
Provisioning infrastructure shouldn’t require every developer to become an expert in Terraform, Bicep, or cloud-specific tooling.

In this talk, I’ll show how Crossplane lets you treat infrastructure like any other Kubernetes resource—making it significantly more accessible for developers while still enforcing platform-level guardrails.

We’ll start with a quick introduction to what Crossplane is and how it works, before diving into real-world usage based on a year of platform engineering experience. You’ll see live demos of provisioning both individual resources (like a virtual network in Azure) and complete landing zones through simple, developer-friendly APIs.

The focus is on Azure, but the patterns and concepts apply equally to AWS, GCP, and hybrid environments.

This session is for developers and platform engineers who want to simplify infrastructure provisioning, reduce cognitive load for developers, and move towards a more self-service, platform-driven model.
Along the way, we’ll also explore lessons learned, trade-offs, and where Crossplane fits—and doesn’t fit—in a modern platform engineering setup.
sessions.title: Stop writing Terraform: Build developer-friendly platforms with Crossplane

sessions.intendedAudience: Software engineers that work with AI agents and suffer from those anti-patterns
sessions.abstract:
Your agent has 100k tokens of context. It still forgets what you told it two messages ago. Prompt "engineering" taught us to craft the perfect instruction. Context engineering treats everything your agent knows as an engineering problem: what it sees, how it retrieves it, what it remembers, and how you prove any of it works.

This talk dissects four antipatterns killing your AI agents and the architectural fixes that actually work:

*  The Stuffed Prompt: You crammed everything upfront and hoped for the best. Static context doesn't scale. Dynamic loading and context refinement, fetching what's needed when it's needed, keeps you within your context window without losing signal. And yes, position matters: models do lose track of what's buried in the middle.

* The Wrong Tool for the Job: You picked one retrieval method and used it everywhere. RAG isn't always the answer. Neither are tools. Neither is an exact match. When do embeddings help, when does MCP give you precision, and when does a simple lookup beat both?

* The Goldfish Agent: Your AI agent forgets everything between sessions. Or worse, remembers everything forever. Short-term and long-term memory, pruning and compaction strategies: what to persist, what to summarize, where to store it, and when to let go.

* The Vibes Eval: You shipped because it "felt right." You can't improve what you don't measure. Eval strategies that prove your context choices work — or expose the tokens you're wasting.

Your context window called. It wants its tokens back.

Bonus: Baruch uses a coding agent to demonstrate these patterns live, so you'll see how they work under the hood — but everything applies to AI agents in general.
sessions.title: The Right 300 Tokens Beat 100k Noisy Ones: Four Context Antipatterns That Kill Your AI Agent

sessions.intendedAudience: Technology leaders, architects and developers
sessions.abstract:
While heavyweight technologies such as Kubernetes and Backstage are often touted as necessary ingredients for an internal developer platform, their flexibility and complexity can easily turn into costly maintenance nightmares, disgruntled developers, or platforms that never see the light of day.

In this talk I’m going to shed light on an alternative, lightweight approach to building internal developer platforms on Amazon Web Services (AWS) that colleagues and I have applied at mid-size companies to boost delivery velocity, get development teams productive on day 1, and keep the platform lean and evolvable over time -- all supported by a tiny platform team, a lot of high-level managed services in the cloud, and zero Kubernetes. The examples are rooted in AWS, but the principles and patterns are broadly applicable.

How did we do this?

Swing by to hear me share key principles, patterns and practices behind the approach; the technologies and AWS services that mattered most; pitfalls to avoid; a blueprint for launching your own lean, internal developer platform; and a brief look at how this approach holds up in the age of AI and evolving demands for digital sovereignty.

You’ll leave with new ideas and perspectives to steer your next platform effort. Without the bloat.
sessions.title: The lightweight approach to building internal developer platforms

sessions.intendedAudience: Anyone who is interested to know more about Nix to setup their development environment.
sessions.abstract: We’ve all said it: "It works on my machine." Docker solved this by shipping the machine, but it didn't necessarily make the build reproducible, just the artifact. Enter Nix: a functional package manager that treats builds like pure mathematical functions. In this 20-minute talk, we’ll compare the "Imperative vs. Functional" approaches to environment management, discuss why Docker images often drift over time, and see how Nix provides bit-for-bit reproducibility. You’ll walk away knowing when to stick with a Dockerfile and when to reach for a Nix Flake.
sessions.title: Reproducible Environments: Why Docker Isn’t Enough and Why Nix Might Be!

sessions.intendedAudience: Primary for Backend Developers and Architects familiar with the JVM (Java/Kotlin). Secondary for Team leads looking to reduce onboarding complexity and "framework magic" in their microservices. We will assume basic knowledge of Spring/DI concepts.
sessions.abstract:
For over a decade, Spring Boot has been the JVM’s undisputed king, promising high-speed delivery through its powerful "magic." But for many developers, that magic eventually becomes a black box. We’ve all been there: a @Transactional that won't roll back, a Bean conflict that takes hours to resolve, or a stack trace that disappears into 50 layers of proxy logic. As applications grow, the implicit "magic" that once helped us becomes a wall—one that’s nearly impossible to climb because we didn't build it.

In this session, we explore a powerful, 100% Kotlin-native alternative: The JetBrains Stack. We will live-build an API using Ktor for asynchronous routing, Exposed for type-safe persistence, and Koin for pragmatic dependency injection, shifting the philosophy from "Convention over Configuration" to "Explicit over Implicit."

What you will learn:
- Explicit over Implicit: Why DSL-based configuration beats annotation scanning for long-term maintainability.
- The Coroutine Advantage: How Ktor leverages Kotlin Coroutines to handle high concurrency with minimal resources.
- Type-Safe Persistence: Using Exposed to write SQL that looks like Kotlin and fails at compile-time, not runtime.
- Lightweight DI: How Koin provides the benefits of Dependency Injection without the reflection and proxy overhead of Spring.

Leave the "black box" behind and discover how to build backends where you—the developer—are actually in control.
sessions.title: No More Magic: Mastering the Explicit Kotlin Stack with Ktor, Exposed and Koin
sessions.suggestedKeywords: Kotlin, Ktor, Exposed, Koin, Backend Architecture, DX, Developer Experience, Microservices

sessions.intendedAudience: developers, architects,devops
sessions.abstract:
Most AI agent tutorials stop at "call the API and print the response." That's not an agent — that's autocomplete with better marketing.

In this workshop you build a real agent system: three services in Docker that work together so the AI can discover and call external tools on its own. The protocol that makes this work is MCP (Model Context Protocol), the open standard from Anthropic that's already integrated into Claude Desktop, VS Code, and Cursor.

You'll build a chat UI, an orchestration layer (Python/FastAPI + OpenAI), and an MCP server where you register your own tools. The agent discovers what's available at startup via JSON-RPC — add a tool, restart, it picks it up. No glue code.

After a short architecture intro, you code. Environment runs in GitHub Codespaces, so setup is a fork and a docker compose up. You'll build two tools from scratch, debug failures, and watch the agent combine multiple tools in one response.

The workshop uses Python, but the MCP protocol and JSON-RPC patterns are language-agnostic — they map directly to any JVM language. For developers and architects who want to understand how AI tool-calling works under the hood, not just consume an API.

Bring a GitHub account and basic programming skills. You leave with a running system to fork and extend.
sessions.title: Build AI Agents That Actually Do Things — Hands-on with MCP

sessions.intendedAudience: Developers and others with curiosity for the difference between Java and Kotlin. No experience needed
sessions.abstract:
In this talk, I share a hands-on, opinionated journey from Kotlin’s concise, modern syntax back to Java’s more explicit (and sometimes verbose) world. Rumor has it that this can be a painful transition—but is Java still what it used to be, or have the changes over the past five years turned the developer experience into something far more enjoyable?

We’ll look at real-world code examples, explore differences in language philosophies, and cover practical dos and don’ts for developers making the same transition. You will get insight into my experience making this shift in a large-scale project at the Norwegian Police.

If you’ve ever wondered whether going from Kotlin to Java is doable—or if you should run the other way—this session gives you a pretty good idea of which way to run.

sessions.intendedAudience: Dette kurset passer for utviklere og andre teknisk interesserte som er nye til Kubernetes, eller som kun har vært så vidt borti det tidligere. Kurset egner seg godt for deltakere som har hørt om Kubernetes, kanskje sett det i bruk i et prosjekt eller i produksjon, men som ikke føler at de helt forstår hva det er eller hvordan delene henger sammen.
sessions.abstract:
Kubernetes har blitt en sentral del av moderne applikasjonsdrift, men for mange oppleves det som komplekst, utilgjengelig og fullt av begreper som er vanskelige å sette i sammenheng. Dette kurset er laget for å senke terskelen og gi en trygg inngang til Kubernetes.

I løpet av kurset lærer du mer om hva Kubernetes er og hvilke problemer det er ment å løse. Vi ser på de viktigste byggesteinene, som containere, pods, services og deployments, og forklarer hvordan de henger sammen i praksis. Fokus ligger på forståelse fremfor konfigurasjonsdetaljer, slik at deltakerne får et mentalt kart de kan bruke videre.

Kurset retter seg mot utviklere og tekniske roller som er nye til Kubernetes, eller som har vært så vidt borti det uten å føle seg helt trygge. Det kreves ingen tidligere erfaring med Kubernetes, kun en grunnleggende forståelse av programvareutvikling og moderne applikasjoner.

Målet er at deltakerne etter kurset skal forstå hva som faktisk skjer når en applikasjon kjøres i Kubernetes, kunne delta i tekniske diskusjoner med større trygghet, og vite hvor de skal gå videre for å lære mer eller komme i gang selv. Kurset gir et solid fundament for videre utforsking av Kubernetes, enten i eget prosjekt eller i samarbeid med andre.
sessions.title: Pleesah, lær meg mer om Kubernetes!

sessions.intendedAudience: Backend and full-stack developers who expose REST APIs and maintain clients across teams or languages. No prior OpenAPI experience needed — basic REST knowledge is enough. Especially valuable for teams where the API and its consumers are owned by different people.
sessions.abstract:
Every team has that one API client that someone wrote 18 months ago. And every time the backend changes, someone has to remember to update it manually — and they don't.

OpenAPI + openapi-generator solves this completely. Your framework (Spring, Micronaut, FastAPI) already generates an OpenAPI spec at build time. One config file and one command later, you have a fully typed, auto-generated SDK in Python, TypeScript, Java, Go, or Kotlin — with Pydantic models, typed method signatures, and proper serialization. No hand-writing. No drift.

This talk shows the full pipeline end-to-end: how the spec is generated, how to configure the generator, what the output looks like, and how to wire it into your build process so the client is always in sync. We'll also cover the honest part — where generators fall short and how a small post-processing script handles the gaps.
sessions.title: Stop Writing API Clients — Let the Spec Do It
