sessions.intendedAudience: Developers (frontend, fullstack). Will benefit to see that focusing on HTML and CSS can give boosts in performance and DX. No experience needed
sessions.abstract:
Våren 2024 skulle Designsystemet komme i versjon 1. Dette skjedde ikkje.
Kvifor? Fordi me var misfornøgde med at det var kun React fokus – i noko som skulle hjelpe alle.
Fant me ein perfekt løysing? Nei.
Kunne me gjere noko som skifta fokuset litt bort frå React? Ja, sett CSS og DOM i fokus.
Arbeidet gjorde at me utsatte versjon 1 med eitt heilt år.
Kva inngjekk i arbeidet med å rydde opp i alt rusket som me fekk av React? Jo, det blei ryddigare DOM, meir fokus på HTML, og ein god del ryddigare CSS.
Denne talken vil raskt gå over korleis me jobba for å få til dette, og kva fordelar me fekk ved å fokusere på CSS og DOM.
sessions.title: Korleis me gjekk frå React til CSS hos Designsystemet

speakers.name: Tobias Barsnes
speakers.bio: Frontend developer from the Norwegian Digitalisation Agency. I strive to make accessible and performant web applications.

sessions.intendedAudience: Alle som er interessert i offentlig forvaltning, internkontroll og er nysgjerrige på hva som foregår i Nav.
sessions.abstract: Hans Christian Holte gikk av som NAV-direktør i 11. november 2025. Bakgrunnen var kritikk fra Riksrevisjonen, blant annet for manglende audit-logging. I denne presentasjonen går vi gjennom hvordan dette henger sammen sett fra teknisk perspektiv. Vi ser også på hvilke konsekvenser det har fått, friksjon rundt etterlevelse og tilnærmingen til innebygd etterlevelse og internkontroll i Nav.
sessions.title: Hvordan logger kan felle en Nav-direktør

speakers.name: Trond Arve Wasskog
speakers.bio: Trond Arve har endelig blitt kvalifisert til en jobb i Nav etter 27 år som konsulent. Etter at han ble ansatt som principal engineer, har han stort sett jobbet modernisering Dagpenger og Arbeidsavklaringspenger. Glad i folk, team og produktutvikling.

speakers.name: Audun Fauchald Strand
speakers.bio: Audun pleide å være principal engineer i NAV. Dermed fikkhan oppfylt drømmen sin om å få kode og bestemme samtidig. Han blir glad av utviklingsfart og tullete navn, og elsker å høre om når ting gikk galt. Nå er han desverre blitt direktør.

sessions.intendedAudience: data engineers and other data practitioners, some familiarity with Python is preferred
sessions.abstract:
Knowledge engineering often feels like a big leap for data engineers: new concepts, new tooling, and unfamiliar terminology.

In this talk, I show that it is actually quite a small leap to get started! By using well-known data engineering tooling, I'll demonstrate how you can easily acquire skills in knowledge engineering. From data frames, pipelines, and transformations--we’ll walk through how the same data can be expressed as a knowledge graph with minimal extra complexity.

The focus is practical: how to think in relationships instead of tables, how to get started quickly, and when knowledge graphs actually add value. This is not a deep dive into theory, but a hands-on introduction for data engineers who are curious about knowledge engineering and want to try it now, not someday.
sessions.title: From Data Engineering to Knowledge Engineering in the blink of an eye
sessions.suggestedKeywords: data engineering, knowledge engineering, knowledge graphs, AI

speakers.name: Veronika Heimsbakk
speakers.bio:
Veronika Heimsbakk is a knowledge graph specialist at Data Treehouse, where she develops technology for making knowledge graphs practical and approachable for data engineers, among others. She enjoys working with messy, unstructured data and turning it into semantic structures that both humans and machines can reason with.

She is the author of SHACL for the Practitioner and was awarded among the Top 50 Women in Tech Norway 2024.

sessions.intendedAudience: Anyone interested in the history of the Java and how we ended up with the Java we have
sessions.abstract:
Using Java as an everyday language can be absolutely infuriating. It's verbose and clunky, with all roads seemingly pointing to null. These are faults that users of other languages (especially of C#) love to point out.

At the same time,  Java is mature, stable, backwards compatible, and runs just about anywhere. The community is pretty cool too!

This talk takes a light-hearted, warts-and-all look at some of the more frustrating aspects of Java, how the language has evolved over time and where it's headed next.

Expect to laugh, and yes maybe even cry, as we try to make sense of the beast that puts food on the table for millions of developers worldwide.

We will cover:
- Pivotal early design decisions such as checked exceptions and generics and how we still pay for those decisions today (that is, why do lambdas suck so bad?)
- How Java has influenced the development of other programming languages, and vice versa
- Most controversial language design decisions of late and the associated fallouts

Java developers will leave this session with a better understanding of the consequences of language design decisions, along with a renewed love for the language that keeps a large chunk of the world running.
sessions.title: 10 Things I Hate About Java

speakers.name: Adele Carpenter
speakers.bio:
Adele is a Software Engineer and Consultant at Trifork Amsterdam where she is working on systems for the educational sector. Most of her work day is spent in the JVM/Spring and React ecosystems, although increasingly she plays a pivotal role as trusted advisor to Trifork’s customers.

Adele is an experienced international speaker, having spoken at multiple editions of NDC, goto, Devoxx and JavaZone. As a speaker, she uses her exposure to real-world customer projects, experiences outside of tech and passion for story-telling to distill complex ideas into their essential parts. All with an air of good humour.

When she’s not at her computer or on stage, you can find her in the gym pumping some serious iron as she pursues the sport of powerlifting.

sessions.intendedAudience: Enhver som noensinne har jobbet med erstatningssystemer, og de som kanskje skulle ønske å unngå å ende opp med det.
sessions.abstract:
For over 50 år siden beskrev Frederick P. Brooks det han kalte "The Second System Effect", hvor uerfarne arkitekter overdriver, går amok og bygger systemer med overdreven bruk av generaliseringer og abstraksjoner, overlesset med unødvendig funksjonalitet. Nesten 25 år seinere kom Brian Foote og Joseph Yoder opp med begrepet "The Big Ball of Mud" - som beskriver hvordan programvare gjerne ender opp når den ikke blir tilstrekkelig vedlikeholdt, et uhåndterlig villnis.

Med så mange års erfaring med å bygge systemer, og med så mye litteratur og kunnskap vi sitter på, burde vi ikke klare å unngå slike villnis? Likevel finnes de overalt, og alle som jobber med å bygge programvare kommer borti dem, igjen og igjen. Manglende dokumentasjon og tester, høy kompleksitet, utgåtte biblioteker og utdaterte best practices. Og de som jobber med dem har neppe vært med på å bygge dem. Som trommene fra dypet i Tolkiens Moria stiger det unisone ropet om at vi må kvitte oss med denne utdaterte, grisete, uforståelige og idiotiske gjørmeballen. Den er en hengemyr med negativ verdi, vi må kaste den og starte på nytt!

Dette er alltid (så godt som) feil!

Grunnen til at programvare blir uhåndterlig handler ikke om at de som har skrevet den er udugelige eller late, det handler om at organisasjonene den blir til i ikke får det til på andre måter. Hvis du ikke klarer å få kontroll på den eksisterende kodebasen din klarer du heller ikke å gjøre det bedre om du starter på nytt.

Det er ikke koden din, men organisasjonen din, som må fikses.

Og det er alltid (så godt som) lettere, billigere og riktigere å fikse det du har!
sessions.title: Erstatningssystemfella

speakers.name: Fredrik de Vibe
speakers.bio: Fredrik har mer enn 20 års erfaring med å lage programvare, med bred erfaring fra forskjellige organisasjoner og teknologier, og han trives best i skjæringspunktet mellom kode og folk. Glad i å snakke og kan lett la seg rive med i diskusjoner om arkitektur, prosess eller omtrent hva som helst ellers, men kommer ikke nærmere Nirvana enn når brikkene begynner å falle på plass og en kompleks problemstilling omsettes i en elegant løsning.

sessions.intendedAudience: Developers excited about performance.
sessions.abstract:
Are you curious about lock-free programming but only familiar with using locks in concurrent Java? This talk is for you! We'll make the concepts of lock-free programming approachable, starting with why it matters and the basics of progress guarantees. You’ll learn to differentiate between wait-freedom, lock-freedom, and obstruction-freedom, understanding why these guarantees are crucial.
We’ll explore essential lock-free programming idioms, like Compare-And-Swap (CAS) for atomic updates, markable references for efficient state management, and the sentinel node technique for simplifying concurrent data structures.
The infamous ABA problem will be demystified—what it is, why it’s problematic, and how to tackle it with techniques like versioning.
To see these concepts in action, we’ll implement three lock-free data structures in Java: a stack, a queue, and a linked list. These examples will highlight the practical application of lock-free techniques and the challenges they address.
Finally, we’ll discuss performance characteristics, comparing the benefits and limitations of lock-free programming versus traditional locking. You’ll learn when to use lock-free methods for better scalability and when sticking with locks might be more appropriate.
Join us to elevate your concurrent programming skills and confidently start experimenting with lock-free programming in your Java projects!
sessions.title: Gentle Introduction to Lock-Free Programming in Java

speakers.name: Andrzej Grzesik
speakers.bio: ags likes distributed systems in all shapes and form. Coding since the age of 8, loves simplicity and continuous delivery. While he has written in many languages, he favours the JVM. Since „most software problems are people problems”, he stirs communities, organizes and speaks at conferences (proud to be a JavaONE Rockstar!). He is passionate about all things data, because science! In his spare time… cycling, photography and books. And he is a Java Champion!

speakers.name: Jarosław Pałka
speakers.bio:
For more than 20 years in the IT industry, as a database administrator, programmer, architect, manager, and “onsite disaster engineer”. I took part in small, medium, and nonsensically large projects carried out according to the rules “Waterfall”, Agile and in the absence of any methodologies, with the same always effect. Which led me to the conclusion that it doesn’t matter what you do as long as you do it in the simplest possible way and use the right tools to do the work for you. Recently I spent too much time exploring JVM’s best-kept secrets, reading about managed runtimes, parsers, and interpreters.

From time to time, you can hear my low-quality jokes about architecture at conferences in Poland.
I am also the author of a blog on http://geekyprimitives.com/, the occasional dictator in conferences program committees, brand, and full-time benchmarking infrastructure engineer at Neo4j.

sessions.intendedAudience: Developers
sessions.abstract:
Let's experience a web that's not made for you!

This talk is not a checklist of rules. It’s an interactive wake-up call.
15–25% of the population needs websites to accommodate different types of disabilities. Still, 94.8% of websites contain accessibility issues that make them harder to use.

It’s not because we don’t want to develop accessible websites and applications.
But we often lack knowledge and perspective. We build based on our own experience because that’s what we know. In this talk, you’ll get to experience how our applications work for someone with a screen reader, low vision, or limited motor control, and feel firsthand the challenges they face.

The talk combines knowledge, practice, and lived experience to raise your awareness of accessibility on the web and how small changes can make a huge difference.

You'll see the most common mistakes in action, who they affect, and how to fix them. So come experience the world through someone else's senses, and learn how to be a more accessible developer.
sessions.title: Let's break some WCAG rules

speakers.name: Elise Kristiansen
speakers.bio:
Elise is a frontend developer in Variant and has been involved in a diverse range of projects for nine years. As a consultant, she has worked in and out of various teams, experiencing a wide range of team compositions.

She has a special interest and passion for accessibility.

sessions.intendedAudience: Designere og utviklere
sessions.abstract: Med 3.8 millioner brukere og 40.000 bedriftskunder, har Digipost en helt unik posisjon til å utforske digitale lommebøker i Norge. Det siste året har teamet jobbet sammen med andre aktører og hatt piloter i sandkassen til Digdir. Brukerhistorier innen sertifisering og organisasjonsidentitet har vært fokuset, som teamet har videreutviklet lommebok-konsepter; en ny måte for virksomheter å dele bevis digitalt, på en trygg måte og på tvers av landegrenser. Målet er å følge bølgen som kommer i Europa med EU-forordningen eIDAS 2.0 (Electronic Identification and Trust Services) om at alle EU/EØS-land skal tilby en offentlig utstedt eID på høyt sikkerhetsnivå. Vi deler erfaringer fra pilotene vi har gjennomført og presenterer prosessen vi har hatt, når vi utvikler produkter for en bruker som ikke finnes enda.
sessions.title: Digipost om UX i Norges nye virksomhetslommebok

speakers.name: Andrea Lysgaard
speakers.bio: Andrea er konsulent hos Kantega og jobber nå med digitale lommebøker hos Digipost. Hun har spesialisert seg innen UX forskning og tjenestedesign, der hun følger videreutvikling fra hypotese til produksjon. Hun har stått på scenen til både på Yggdrasilkonferansen og Booster-konferansen tidligere.

sessions.intendedAudience: Java programmers of all experience levels who are intrigued by the notions of pattern matching and data-oriented programming
sessions.abstract: Java is gaining features to support pattern matching and a "data-oriented" programming style. In this presentation, I explore when this programming style is beneficial, and how you should structure your programs to take advantage of it.  You will learn the syntax of pattern matching, as it has evolved, and you will get a glimpse at further enhancements in upcoming versions of Java. Just as importantly, I will show you how to recognize confusing and error-prone usages of pattern matching, which the new features also enable. You will come away with a mental model when to use pattern matching and "data orientation" now, how to prepare for future enhancements, and when to stay away.
sessions.title: Java Patterns: Why, How, and When Not

speakers.name: Cay Horstmann
speakers.bio: After growing up in the German countryside, I studied in the United States (M.S. in computer science from Syracuse University, and a Ph.D. in mathematics from the University of Michigan in Ann Arbor). For four years, I was VP and CTO of an Internet startup that went from 3 people in a tiny office to a public company. I taught computer science at San Jose State University and held visiting appointments at universities in Germany, Switzerland, Vietnam, and Macau. In my copious spare time I write books and articles on programming languages and computer science education.

sessions.intendedAudience: Developers, architects, tech leads, and IT leaders
sessions.abstract:
This talk examines a quiet enterprise uniformisation accelerated by Azure and Copilot: the gradual replacement of engineering judgment with Microsoft as the default answer to every question. "If it is from Microsoft, we should probably be using it." .NET, SharePoint, Teams, Bicep, Copilot, PowerShell — no engineering evaluation, no cost analysis, no critical thinking. "Following Microsoft best practices" is mistaken for strategy and treated as a silver bullet.

This pattern is not new. Microsoft's ecosystem has traditionally been proprietary, and developers long ago learned to treat its offerings as the safe default and everything else as a risk. That cultural habit has driven the massive adoption of Office 365, Teams, and Azure — not through careful assessment of alternatives, but through a continual default to the path of least resistance.

Microsoft's cloud strategy creates a lock-in far deeper than most organisations realise. Azure alone is manageable. Azure + Office 365 + Entra ID is a triple lock most organisations never see coming. In a European context where digital sovereignty is an active political conversation, this deserves to be named plainly.

The same pattern is now repeating in AI. Copilot is not winning enterprise evaluations — it is bypassing them. It arrives pre-attached to existing EA agreements before anyone has asked a technical question. Capable, enterprise-grade alternatives simply never become visible.

This talk is an eye-opener for organisations that adopt Microsoft products uncritically, even inadequate ones, simply because it is easier contractually or organisationally. Microsoft monoculture means innovating at Microsoft's pace and on Microsoft's terms — whether you chose that or not.
sessions.title: Microsoft Monoculture: The Lock-in Nobody Talks About

speakers.name: Nordine Ben Bachir
speakers.bio: I've spent more than two decades in the industry as a developer, consultant, and architect, long enough to have seen technology shifts arrive with fanfare and depart quietly, leaving organisations to clean up the gap between the promise and the reality. Since 2015 my focus has been on cloud, cloud-native applications, and platform engineering. That work has taken me inside enough organisations to notice patterns that individual projects don't reveal and in particular, how vendor relationships shape technical culture. I'm a Manager at Bekk Consulting in Oslo.

sessions.intendedAudience: Java developers in larger projects
sessions.abstract:
We build a lot of microservices where common functionality is extracted into shared, innersource libraries. We manage 30+ of these libraries used by dozens of downstream applications or other libraries. Sometimes we need to break the API or require new configuration.
In these cases, version bumps alone are rarely enough. Tools like Dependabot can update dependencies, but they cannot refactor code, migrate configuration, or make breaking changes explicit. Engineers are left to read changelogs, hunt through compile errors, and reapply the same fixes across repositories. And, worst of all, many teams will probably need to do the same thing.
That’s why we decided to stop just managing version bumps and instead automate the refactoring itself. We adopted OpenRewrite to define custom recipes that automatically apply changes. Breaking changes became executable migration guides instead of documentation no one reads.
In this talk you will learn how to set up OpenRewrite for internal libraries. Build and test your custom recipes.
sessions.title: Managing the chaos: Automating internal dependency upgrades with OpenRewrite
sessions.suggestedKeywords: OpenRewrite, Build tooling, Maven, Innersource, Library

speakers.name: Jago de Vreede
speakers.bio: Jago is a full-stack software engineer at TenneT, as a software engineer, he has seen a broad spectrum of projects. He also likes to take Java to the extreme and do things with are not normally done with Java, like playing an automated guitar. He is passionate about his work and likes to share his knowledge and has therefore spoken at conferences worldwide.

sessions.intendedAudience: Java developers who want to know how the JVM really works. Ideally, the audience should have some knowledge of class loading. No C++ knowledge will be necessary for this talk.
sessions.abstract:
Have you ever wondered why a particular class was loaded at that exact moment? Getting to the bottom of it can be surprisingly hard.
I recently encountered a bug with a particularly nasty linkage error in which classes were loading unexpectedly, and I needed to figure out what triggered that. In this talk, I’ll walk through the steps I used to track it down. We’ll build a slowdebug JVM, attach GDB, and use breakpoints and inspection to identify the precise cause of the class loading.
If you’re curious about how the JVM really works and want to peek under the hood, this talk is for you.
sessions.title: Debugging Class Loading with GDB

speakers.name: Marco Sussitz
speakers.bio: Marco Sussitz is an engineer at Dynatrace who works at the boundary of Java and C++. He writes JVMTI agents, instruments bytecode, and digs into JIT, memory, and thread behavior. You’ll often find him sorting out class loaders or reading the spec longer than he should. Before Dynatrace, he worked on cloud video encoding with FFmpeg. In his off hours, he’s bouldering or in the mountains.

sessions.intendedAudience: Utviklere og tech leads som jobber med produksjonssystemer og er nysgjerrige på hvordan AI-agenter kan brukes i praksis, utover ren kodegenerering. Du trenger ingen erfaring med OpenTelemetry eller Application Insights; foredraget dekker grunnlaget. Det hjelper å ha jobbet med mikrotjenester og å ha kjent på frustrasjonen av å feilsøke i produksjon.
sessions.abstract:
I fjor viste jeg på JavaZone hvordan OpenTelemetry og Java-agenter gir deg røntgensyn i produksjon. I år viser jeg hva som skjer når du kobler en AI-agent til de samme dataene — og til kildekoden.

Dashboards krever at du vet hva du leter etter. Men hva om du bare kunne spørre? Med AI-agenter som har tilgang til kildekode metrikker og traces fra produksjon kan du stille spørsmål i naturlig språk, grave deg nedover i dataene, og gå rett fra innsikt til handling.

Foredraget starter med en rask oppsummering av observability fra fjorårets foredrag, før vi dykker ned i samtale som grensesnitt mot telemetri, og agenter som kryssrefererer produksjonsinformasjon mot kildekoden din.

Jeg viser eksempler fra reelle sesjoner viser jeg hele løpet. Fra du oppdager «vi har feil i produksjon» til agenten finner årsaken i koden, skriver en fix og lager en pull request.

Foredraget bygger på erfaringer fra over 60 reelle sesjoner med agenter og observability der det kombineres AppInsights-data, kildekode og git-historikk for å løse problemer som ingen av delene kunne løst alene.

Uansett om du så fjorårets foredrag eller er helt ny for temaet, vil jeg vise hvordan AI-agenter kan gjøre produksjonsdataene dine lettere å forstå og hvordan du kan løse problemer kjapt.
sessions.title: Bare spør! AI-agenter elsker produksjonsmetrikker
sessions.suggestedKeywords: OpenTelemetry, AI Agents, Observability, Application Insights, Developer Experience

speakers.name: Anders Sveen
speakers.bio: Anders Sveen har jobbet med utvikling i over 20 år og driver konsulentselskapet Mikill, der han kombinerer utvikling, rådgivning og mentoring. Han brenner for enkel og effektiv utvikling som løser reelle forretningsproblemer, med DevOps, observability, TDD og kontinuerlige leveranser som de viktigste verktøyene. Når AI øker farten på utviklingen er gode utviklingspraksiser bare viktigere. Den siste tiden har han jobbet mye med hvordan AI-agenter kan bli en naturlig del av prosessen, fra koding og testing til å snakke med produksjonsdata.

sessions.intendedAudience: Developers, AI Engineers
sessions.abstract:
The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is becoming the standard for how we connect AI apps to external data sources, but most of the conversation is stuck on "localhost." What happens when you need to take an MCP server out of your terminal and serve it to millions of users? 
In this talk, we bridge the gap between AI development and platform engineering. First, Mohamed will share the developer journey of building Spotify’s very first MCP Server and integrating it with ChatGPT, covering the architectural choices, the code, and the challenges of mapping complex data to the MCP standard. Then, Abdel steps in to tackle the Ops reality: how do you run these servers at scale? We will dive into containerizing MCP servers, deploying them on Kubernetes, and handling the hard parts, such as authentication, rate limiting, observability, and managing concurrent connections from AI agents. 
Whether you are a developer looking to build your first MCP app or a platform engineer tasked with running them, you will leave with a practical blueprint for taking MCP from a local prototype to a highly available, planet-scale service.
sessions.title: Scaling the Context: A Dev & Ops Guide to Running MCP Servers in Production

speakers.name: Abdel Sghiouar
speakers.bio: Abdel Sghiouar is a senior Cloud Developer Advocate @Google Cloud. A co-host of the Kubernetes Podcast by Google and a CNCF Ambassador. His focused areas are GKE/Kubernetes, Service Mesh, and Serverless.

speakers.name: Mohamed Aboullaite
speakers.bio: Mohammed is a community catalyst, a true open-source believer who has contributed to many open-source projects. Mohammed has extensive hands-on, cross-industry experience in designing, building and evolving distributed applications at scale. He’s a Docker Captain, Java champion and one of Google developer experts in cloud technologies, and work @Spotify as Sr Backend engineer.

sessions.intendedAudience: Developers interesting in honing their skills and learn how to be creative with code, algorithms and maths!
sessions.abstract:
Ever heard of the demoscene? It is this amazing community of recreational and competitive programmers. The point is to use code to create the most amazing graphical creations and impress everyone with artistic algorithmic brilliance.

The demoscene taught me so much about being a good programmer, and I thought I'd give you all the opportunity to join in on the fun and learn how to create some great demos! 

I will guide you through the following:
- Old school demo effect concepts
- Modern-day demo effect concepts
- Entry to shadercoding on Directx12
- Tools of the trade
- How to be creative with mathematics
- Bringing it all together to a demo-concept

So bring out your graphic calculators, and let's get those techno beats going and learn how to paint and create worlds with maths!
sessions.title: Demoscene Coding Kickstart

speakers.name: Espen Sande-Larsen
speakers.bio:
Espen is a developer and architect with over 25 years of experience. He has worked on everything from embedded systems, electronics and games to large-scale cloud applications. He is as full-stack as they come. Espen started coding at age six on a C64, and has been cranking out software ever since.

He served as the principal architect, tech lead and lead security
engineer on the compensation scheme for Covid-19 suffering
businesses released by the Norwegian government.

Espen was the Senior VP of Technology Exploration for DNB in San Francisco and is currently doing technology and security research in DNB NewTechLab.

sessions.intendedAudience: Backend developers who have experienced service code becoming messy or hard to maintain. Attendeed will leave with a simple, practical way to structure their code and make it easier to work with.
sessions.abstract:
Service layers often start simple but slowly turn into a mix of business logic, infrastructure calls, and "just one more thing". The result is hard-to-read code, fragile tests, and a structure that depends more on discipline than design.

There are well-known solutions like onion, hexagonal, or clean architecture. They work - but they also require a lot of upfront understanding and ongoing effort to keep intact. In practice, many teams don't fully adopt them.

In this talk, I'll share a small, practical approach I've used to bring structure back into service layers - without introducing heavy patterns or requiring everyone to "buy in" to a big architectural model.

We'll look at a real example, how it evolves, and what changes make the biggest difference. The focus is on something you can apply immediately, even in an existing codebase.
sessions.title: Your Service Layer Is a Mess (Here's a Simple Fix)

speakers.name: Jarle Mathiesen
speakers.bio:
Jarle Mathiesen is a senior consultant at Knowit and has been working as a consultant for 8 years. He is a full stack developer with a strong focus on backend systems.

He cares deeply about architecture, building robust and testable systems, and keeping designs simple. He is also interested in software craftsmanship and how AI agents are changing the way we build software.

sessions.intendedAudience: People looking for ways to optimize startup times of their applications
sessions.abstract: Docker is the cornerstone of today's development! Let's overview quickly how we use it in JVM development, and then transition to practical examples. We will start with a simple Docker image containing a popular "one fat JAR" and look into its downsides (deployment speed, traffic volume). To address them, we will discuss the strategy of splitting the JAR into multiple layers and using multistage Dockerfiles, gradually improving our image. Join me to learn how to build the ultimate Docker image for Spring applications.
sessions.title: Crafting the Ultimate Docker Image for Spring Applications

speakers.name: Pasha Finkelshteyn
speakers.bio: Years of experience in software engineering and the team's leading roles combined with his passion for Java made Pasha know all the hidden details in this IT niche. As a Developer Advocate for @Bellsoft, he educates the public on the latest software tools built by BellSoft and helps to create and test instruments for developers. He writes in Kotlin, speaks at conferences, composes a new articles, or maintains his pet projects.

sessions.intendedAudience: Java and Spring developers building production AI applications. Spring AI exposure is helpful but not required. No graph database experience needed.
sessions.abstract:
Multi-agent Spring AI systems share data but not reasoning. The reasoning behind each agent's answer has no persistent form, and the next agent inherits the same data and none of the context the others built. This talk presents a Context-Aware Advisor built on the Spring AI Advisor API that acts as the Decision Layer. It intercepts every query, persists a structured decision trace to Neo4j, and resolves subsequent queries through graph-enriched decision search.

The graph is the advantage. Vector search finds decisions that look similar; graph traversal follows the relationships that make a prior decision applicable. A query that surfaces a conflict resolution traverses to the policy that authorized it, the exception that modified it, and every account that resolution has since governed. That structural context is complete grounding: not proximity to a document, but position in the decision record.

The talk ends with a complete multi-agent Spring AI architecture where agents share not just data, but the reasoning behind every decision that came before.
sessions.title: The Decision Layer: Context Graphs for Spring AI
sessions.suggestedKeywords: Spring AI, AI agents, Knowledge Graphs, Semantic layers,  Enterprise AI

speakers.name: James Ward
speakers.bio: Professional software developer since 1997, with much of that time spent helping developers build software that doesn't suck. A Typed Pure Functional Programming zealot who often compromises on his ideals to just get stuff done. Currently a Developer Advocate for AWS.

speakers.name: Ryan Knight
speakers.bio:
Ryan Knight is a Senior Partner Architect at Neo4j, where he works with AWS and Databricks to deliver public workshops and build Enterprise AI agents grounded in knowledge graphs. His work centers on how Knowledge Graphs can provide Enterprise agents the context for both data and decisions, grounded in a background in data engineering and distributed systems.

Prior to Neo4j, Ryan led Grand Cloud, a consultancy delivering data and generative AI solutions for clients ranging from startups to the Fortune 500. He has held senior architecture and advisory roles at Starbucks, Capital One, DataStax, and Lightbend.

sessions.intendedAudience: This talk is targeted at anyone getting into building agentic AI projects, get hands-on experience
sessions.abstract:
In this hands-on workshop, participants will learn how to create and secure AI agents leveraging Quarkus LangChain4j. As AI agents are used more and more within enterprise applications, securing them has become a critical concern. You for example don't want your agent to drop your database, execute privileged actions or leak your sensitive customer data by accident.

Workshop participants will:

- Set up a Quarkus project with LangChain4j extension
- Build a basic AI agent that can interact with business applications
- Implement security measures to prevent prompt injection attacks
- Configure authentication and authorization for AI agent actions
- Structure the application for maintainability and security
- Add appropriate monitoring and audit logging

Each participant should bring their own laptop with JDK 21, Maven, Ollama and Podman Desktop installed. The workshop will include step-by-step instructions, code examples, and exercises to reinforce learning.

This workshop is suitable for developers new to AI and more experienced engineers who want to learn how to include and secure AI agents in their projects. Some basic knowledge of Java and Quarkus is helpful but not required.
sessions.title: Building secure AI agents with Quarkus LangChain4j
sessions.workshopPrerequisites:
Make sure you have the following installed locally:

JDK 25 (you can leverage SDKMAN!)
IntelliJ IDEA or VS Code with the Java & Quarkus extension enabled
Podman Desktop or Docker Desktop
Ollama
Quarkus CLI (optional)

This workshop needs to download a lot of dependencies, so it might take a while on the first run. If you have an unlimited data plan, you can speed up the process by using your mobile connection instead of the shared wireless connection.

To run this workshop you need access to an LLM. You can either use a local model or one of the other providers that you have access to.
Ollama
OpenAI
Gemini

sessions.intendedAudience: Utviklere og arkitekter som jobber mot store eller komplekse domenemodeller, eller som har kjent på smerten ved å presse grafbaserte data inn i relasjonsdatabaser. Du trenger ikke kjenne til OWL eller grafdatabaser fra før, men erfaring med domenedrevet design og typesystemer i Java eller Kotlin gir mest utbytte.
sessions.abstract:
Vi kjenner alle til smerten i overgangen mellom relasjonsdatabaser og objekter. Skaler opp til tusenvis av klasser, relasjoner og constraints fra en internasjonal standard, og du ender med forenklinger og kode som ikke egentlig greier å uttrykke domenet skikkelig. For å strø salt i såret, innse at domenet egentlig ligner mer på en graf enn en haug tabeller.

Vi sto overfor akkurat dette. Standarden vi jobber mot er enorm, grafbasert, og skrevet for og av domeneeksperter med årevis av erfaring. For en utvikler er den nærmest uleselig. Men den er maskinlesbar. I stedet for å redusere standarden til noe en relasjonsdatabase kan håndtere, genererte vi Kotlin-kode direkte fra spesifikasjonen og bygget et tynt, fleksibelt abstrasjonslag mot grafdatabasen.

Resultatet: et felles vokabular mellom utviklere og domeneeksperter, interoperabilitet som følger standarden ut av boksen, og fordelene du forbinder med en liten håndskrevet modell, selv om standarden har tusenvis av klasser. Færre feil, raskere utvikling, og en kompilator og IDE som guider deg gjennom et domene du ikke trenger å forstå fullt ut på forhånd.

Dette er domenedrevet design slik det er ment: domenet uttrykt klart og tydelig, mens infrastruktur-koden ligger ryddig adskilt ved siden av.
sessions.title: Hvordan vi temmer en domenemodell med 1500+ klasser
sessions.suggestedKeywords: Domain-Driven Design, Kotlin, Code Generation, Graph-OOP impedance, Ontology

sessions.intendedAudience: This is for folks, who: have to process loads of data, and/or are intrigued how non-GA modern features make Java shine in prod
sessions.abstract:
Processing large amounts of data efficiently is a common challenge. Many workloads spend most of their time doing simple math on large arrays. Modern CPUs can speed this up using SIMD, where one instruction processes multiple values at once, but using it from Java has traditionally been difficult and required native code.

The Panama Vector API changes this by letting developers express these operations directly in Java, while the JVM translates them into efficient CPU instructions.

Elasticsearch is a real-world example. Features like vector search and ES|QL rely on repeated operations over large datasets. Under the hood, Elasticsearch and Lucene use the Vector API to take advantage of SIMD on modern CPUs.

Using an incubating API in production is unusual. In this case, the performance gains make it worth it. This talk explores how Elasticsearch integrates the Vector API, how it enables or disables SIMD at runtime, and what this means for high performance Java.
sessions.title: SIMD for Java: how Elasticsearch already benefits from the Panama Vector API

sessions.intendedAudience: This talk is aimed at backend developers who are working with, or considering Ktor, and who want a clearer mental model of how Kotlin Coroutines fit into the picture.
sessions.abstract:
JetBrains' Ktor is built from the ground up on Kotlin Coroutines. Yet Coroutines remain one of the more challenging concepts for many Kotlin developers - myself included. For the last 3 years I have been working as a backend developer on a project where we used Ktor. I have picked up both good habits and hard lessons along the way.

This talk focuses on how Coroutines work in Ktor,  and how you can leverage Coroutines effectively in your own projects. While it is possible to use Ktor without thinking much about Coroutines,  a solid understanding can help you write safer,  more efficient,  and more maintainable code.

We will cover the key benefits of Kotlin Coroutines,  how Ktor uses them internally,  and practical patterns for real-world scenarios — including running tasks in parallel,  managing background work,  and handling exceptions correctly.  We will also walk through an example of building a custom Ktor plugin that leverages Coroutines in your application.
sessions.title: Kotlin Coroutines in Ktor: What you need to know
sessions.suggestedKeywords: Kotlin, Coroutines, Kotlin Coroutines, Ktor, Backend

sessions.intendedAudience: Any Java developer will surely find this talk useful since the requirement is very common and the results can be applied on many projects.
sessions.abstract: In this talk, we're going to analyze the best way we can fetch a multi-level hierarchy from an RDBMS so that we can store the result set in a cache, such as Redis. I'm going to go through various solutions and analyze the performance impact of each option. From plain JDBC to JdbcTemplate, Spring Data JDBC, Spring Data JPA, and Blaze. Therefore, we are going to compare how all these options work and conclude what is the most efficient way to fetch a multi-level hierarchical structure with the most common Java data access technologies.
sessions.title: The best way to fetch multi-level hierarchies from a RDBMS using Java
sessions.suggestedKeywords: Java, Spring Data JPA, Hibernate, Blaze Persistence, performance tuning

sessions.intendedAudience: Utviklere som har arvet programvare og trenger inspirasjon til rollen som kode-arkeolog
sessions.abstract:
Har du noen gang arvet kode som gjorde deg motløs og sinna og overbevist om at hele greia må skrotes? Sjappa har ingen planer om å skru av systemet, koden leverer verdi, men fremdrift føles umulig. Vi må begynne på nytt! Med blanke ark! Bygge opp alt fra bånn! Men er vi så sikker på at videreutvikling er håpløst? Går det kanskje an å grave frem det som kan demonteres, identifisere deler som definitivt skal saneres, men også finne ut hvor skattene ligger? Forvandle dette røklet til noe hjemmekoselig og brukbart? Én ting er sikkert - hvis vi ikke tør å utforske det ukjente, så finner vi aldri ut om det er mulig.

Bli med på utgravning i programvare med røtter tilbake til 2002. Vi ser på hvordan OpenTelemetry er vår venn for å observere appene i produksjon. Takk til mise og Compose som gjør det moro å rigge lokalt utviklingsmiljø, også når appen forlanger både lokal PostgreSQL og en SMTP-tjener (!) Vi skal oppgradere hele veien fra Java 8 til Java 25. Og jepp, vi må definitivt besøke Spring Boot Temple of Doom. Vi ser hvordan IntelliJ IDEA hjelper slette kode i industriell skala uten at vi trenger holde pusten. Men vi kommer til å knekke både det ene og det andre. Vi deler arbeidsuhell også, mens vi diskuterer risiko og hvilke grep som gjør dem akseptable. 

Vi forteller hvordan vi kom i gang med det som virket som en tapt kamp. At vi fant motivasjon, trygghet og momentum. Vi tror mange utviklere kan finne glede og nytte i å jobbe med eksisterende kode. Finne ut hvordan bygge videre på noe som fins fra før, selv om utgangspunktet ikke er skrevet av oss. Når ny kode skrives fortere og fortere, så tror vi at behovet for å kunne håndtere ukjent programvare bare blir større. Hvilke ferdigheter trenger en moderne kode-arkeolog?
sessions.title: Kodearkeologer på legacy eventyr

sessions.intendedAudience: Senior Java developers and tech leads who already use AI coding tools and are responsible for code quality   and team standards. No introduction to AI tools — this session starts where the marketing ends.
sessions.abstract:
Something uncomfortable is happening to Java developers right now. You're shipping more code than ever. Your PRs are larger. Your velocity metrics look great. And somewhere between the third suggestion you accepted without reading and the afternoon you spent debugging code you didn't fully write, a question starts forming: am I getting better at this, or worse?

We call this Fear-Driven Development. Not the deadline-pressure kind — something subtler. The growing unease that your tools are making you faster while making you weaker.

This talk is built around that fear. And around six systems we built in response to it.

Over the past year, those systems — collectively called Skill-Driven Development — were tested at production scale: a 197,000-line Java library in eleven days, no existing libraries in the domain, 7,461 tests at 99.8% pass rate. And more recently, in production SaaS systems where the same discipline caught AI-generated code that passed all tests but contained subtle GDPR retention logic errors and binary encoding mistakes that would have shipped in any copy-paste workflow.

The intent here is to move to a BOF format where thesession is interactive. We'll work through real examples: skill files, verification checklists, and the actual failure modes — code that looked correct but wasn't. You'll leave with a concrete framework you can apply starting Monday.

Format: workshop, small group, discussion-based. Bring your actual questions — the ones you haven't been able to ask out loud.
sessions.title: Trust, But Verify: Skill-Driven Development for the Sceptical Java Developer
sessions.suggestedKeywords: Skill-Driven Development · verification · Java · software craftsmanship · AI tools

speakers.name: Totto - Thor Henning Hetland
speakers.bio:
Thor Henning Hetland (Totto) has been writing Java professionally for 30 years — before generics, before  lambdas, before the JVM had a JIT. He co-founded JavaZone in 2002.

  Today he runs eXOReaction, a software architecture practice, and is CTO at Mynder, a Norwegian compliance SaaS. He built Synthesis, an open-source knowledge infrastructure platform used across large Java codebases, and the Knowledge Context Protocol (KCP), a proposed standard for structured agent context.

  The lib-pcb project — 197,831 lines of Java, 7,461 tests, eleven days — is the concrete answer to the question this workshop asks. His expertise grew stronger through the process, not weaker. That's the point.

sessions.intendedAudience: Java developers working with data pipelines, data engineers managing ETL/ELT processes, backend engineers evaluating lakehouse architectures. Attendees should understand basic SQL and Kafka concepts. Familiarity with Kubernetes is helpful but not required.
sessions.abstract:
Batch ETL runs nightly. Your analysts query stale data. Your ML models train on yesterday's features. The streaming-first lakehouse replaces all of that with a single real-time pipeline, and you can build it entirely with open-source tools.

In this talk I'll demo a complete pipeline: Debezium captures row-level changes from PostgreSQL, streams them through Kafka with schemas enforced by Apicurio Registry (a CNCF sandbox project), and lands them in Apache Iceberg tables, queryable within seconds via Trino or Spark. I'll cover how automatic schema evolution works across the Kafka-to-Iceberg boundary, eliminating the manual DDL changes that plague traditional data lakes.

You'll see a column added to PostgreSQL automatically propagate through Debezium, get versioned in Apicurio Registry with BACKWARD compatibility, and update the Iceberg table schema, all without manual intervention. I'll also demonstrate Iceberg's time-travel queries for ML reproducibility.
sessions.title: From Database to Lakehouse in Real-Time: CDC, Kafka, Apicurio, and Apache Iceberg
sessions.suggestedKeywords: Apache Iceberg, Change Data Capture, Kafka, Real-time Analytics, Lakehouse

Teknologi kan ikkje forståast i isolasjon. For å forstå teknologien må me også forstå korleis hjernen vår fungerer. Heilt sidan teknologi blei ei greie så har menneskeleg ekspertise mista terreng til teknologien, men om me ser alt med teknologibrillene på så er me blinde for dei problema som ikkje let seg reduserast til tekniske løysningar. Då risikerer me å laga produkt som ingen intelligens, korkje kunstig eller organisk, kan forstå eller rydda opp i.

Dette foredraget vil gje deg eit vokabular og rammeverk for å diskutera læring i kontekst av programvareutvikling. Me vil gjera det ved å utforska to sentrale tema:
- Kva typar problem blir best utforska av mennesker?
- Korleis prosessar, verkty og koden me skriv påverker evna vår til å læra og til å løysa problem, og kva me kan gjera for å betra denne evna.

Me kjem til å utforska tema som: taus og eksplisitt kunnskap, kvifor folkepsykologi ofte tek feil, teori om kognitiv last, og effekten av kognitiv avlastning.

sessions.intendedAudience: Developers, architects, tech leads, product owners, managers. No experience required.
sessions.abstract:
Dei fleste produktteam måler suksess i vekst, brukartal og nye tekniske eigenskapar. Teamet vårt vil heller måla suksess i kor mange som har slutta å bruka løysingane våre.

I 2015 bygde Difi (no Digdir) ein "transisjonsarkitektur" for meldingsutveksling i offentleg sektor. Peppol-nettverket vart sett på som framtida, men infrastrukturen var ikkje heilt klar. Så me bygde ei bru, som etter kvart fekk eit namn (eFormidling), eit team, eit vegkart, og ein plass i portefølja av digitale fellesløysingar.

Men midlertidige løysingar som fungerar har gjerne ein tendens til å verta meir og meir permanente. Gode innspel kjem inn, vegkartet veks, teamet susar avgårde, og eit viktig premiss glimrar med sitt fråvær: avvikling.

I 2020 tok me fram dei gamle rapportane våre og spurde: Er Peppol klar no? Svaret var ja. Så me gjorde noko uvant, me byrja å planlegga avvikling av løysinga vår. Me feirar kvar gong me skrur av ein integrasjon.

Korleis kan ein gjera End of Life til eit mål i seg sjølv? Korleis held ein teamet motivert når roadmapen peikar mot ingenting? Og kvifor var det å skriva "transisjonsarkitektur" i ein rapport frå 2015 det smartaste teamet vårt gjorde?

Av og til er det beste suksesskriteriet kor elegant ein klarar å forsvinna.
sessions.title: Sunset as a Service: Når målet er EOL

sessions.intendedAudience: Attendees will walk away with not just understanding, but confidence—and a new ability to write safer, more expressive code. Moderate experience with Java is recommended.
sessions.abstract:
You might think Java is type-safe, but did you know you can *break* type safety too? It all comes down to variance: co-, contra-, and invariance.  

Maybe you’ve heard of these terms, but you’re not entirely sure what they mean or how to use them effectively. That was exactly my situation — even after more than ten years of experience with Java. I’ve noticed this same knowledge gap among the students I teach.  

Type safety gives us reliability but also adds complexity compared to weakly, dynamically typed languages. Why do we even have types? What makes something a subtype? Do you think you can pass an `Integer` into a parameter of type `Number`? And what about a “type that has a type”? Generics are Java’s way of handling such cases, but they can introduce unexpected and sometimes seemingly illogical behavior. For example: Can you pass a `List<Integer>` into a parameter of type `List<Number>`? What is a capture, and why do you sometimes see errors like "`add(capture of ? extends A)` cannot be applied to `S`"?  

In this talk, I’ll demystify these concepts and explain them in a way you’ll never forget. You’ll impress your colleagues, gain a competitive edge, and be better prepared to understand and write reusable (framework) code.
sessions.title: Generics – You Never Know What You’re Gonna Get
sessions.suggestedKeywords: generics, type erasure, covariant, contravariant, subtype

sessions.intendedAudience: People who used basic feature flags and want to learn how the field evolved into Feature Management
sessions.abstract:
Basic feature flags gave teams on/off control over what users see, decoupling code deployments from feature releases.
More advanced targeted flags let us release the right features, to the right people, at the right time.
But in 2026, the challenge isn't toggling — it's managing the entire feature lifecycle from idea to impact.
Packed with lessons from production, live demos, and a healthy dose of engineering humor - you’ll learn:
* How to build runtime feature management into your architecture
* How to find bottlenecks in your feature lifecycle
* How to make releases consistently boring
* How to make releases that monitor themselves
sessions.title: Feature Management: Beyond Feature Flags
sessions.suggestedKeywords: feature flags, feature management, feature lifecycle, safer releases

sessions.intendedAudience: This has something for everyone
sessions.abstract:
Ted Nelson was a troublemaker. In 1974, frustrated by the closed and bureaucratic world of computing, he wrote Computer Lib/Dream Machines, a book that was part manifesto and part blueprint for a digital future. Nelson saw computers not as tools of control but as portals to personal freedom, creativity and interconnected knowledge. He championed hypertext long before the web, called out the dangers of technical jargon designed to exclude ordinary people and fought for systems that put users in charge.

This talk tells the story of Computer Lib, the man behind it and the philosophy that shaped it. It explores how Nelson’s ideas could have led to a very different digital world, one built around open, decentralised knowledge rather than the walled gardens and algorithmic control we have today. What if his vision had won? What would the internet look like if computing had followed the path of Computer Lib instead of Big Tech?

Nelson once wrote that “most authority is malignant.” This is a talk about the man who wanted to dismantle it and the future we lost when the world ignored him.
sessions.title: Dream Machines & Walled Gardens

sessions.intendedAudience: Platform and DevOps engineers tired of maintaining forked Terraform modules, security engineers writing OPA or Sentinel who want policies that do more than just fail plans, and infra leads looking to enforce standards across teams
sessions.abstract:
Every org enforcing Terraform standards eventually hits the same wall: policy tools can flag issues, but they cannot fix module code. The result is a graveyard of forked modules that drift from upstream and turn upgrades into a full-time job. This talk introduces policy transformation: automatically rewriting Terraform modules at download time so teams get compliant code without maintaining forks.

I will demo eight real transformation rules across four categories: lifecycle management (prevent_destroy, ignore tag drift, protect KMS keys), block removal (strip provisioners), attribute restriction (deny GPU or specialty instances), and content sanitization (safe regex cleanups). You will see the before-and-after HCL, plus the safety model that makes this production-ready: deterministic outputs, collision detection, preview diffs, and a four-level risk classification.

You will leave with a practical decision framework for validate vs transform, a DIY toolkit using pre-commit, hclwrite, custom tflint rules, and plan validation, and a simple migration path from module forks to rule-based enforcement. No vendor account required to apply the patterns from this talk.
sessions.title: No More Forks: Policy Transformation for Terraform at Scale

speakers.name: Anton Babenko
speakers.bio:
Anton is an AWS Community Hero and helps companies around the globe build solutions using AWS, and specializes in infrastructure-as-code, DevOps, and reusable infrastructure components.

He spends much of his time as an open-source contributor on various Terraform & AWS projects, such as Terraform AWS modules (downloaded more than 2 billion times), Terraform best practices ebook (www.terraform-best-practices.com), doing serverless with Terraform (serverless.tf), Terraform Weekly (weekly.tf), Your Weekly Dose of Terraform (http://bit.ly/terraform-youtube).

sessions.intendedAudience: Moderately experienced developers and up. People curious about exploring different languages.
sessions.abstract:
Are you interested in how the world works outside of the JVM or the Java ecosystem as a whole? How would one work with a language that has no exceptions? What about working in an ecosystem where the mantra is "let it crash"?

Gleam is a statically-typed, functional, immutable language that runs on the BEAM VM—a VM where every process is tiny, isolated, and communicates via messaging, and that can even run across a cluster. And if that’s not enough, Gleam can also compile to JavaScript.

Join me as we explore a different language on a different VM, discover what makes the BEAM unique, and see how Gleam brings strong typing and modern syntax to this powerful ecosystem.
sessions.title: Gleam and BEAM: Looking beyond the JVM
sessions.suggestedKeywords: Functional programming, Distributed applications, Gleam, BEAM

speakers.name: John Mikael Lindbakk
speakers.bio:
John Mikael Lindbakk is a tech lead, backend architect, and developer with a decade of experience building scalable, maintainable software. He focuses on clean architecture, automated testing, and developer processes across industries like banking, healthcare, and insurance.

Passionate about code quality and thoughtful design, John Mikael enjoys sharing his views and discoveries through his writing and presentations.

sessions.intendedAudience: Developers, architects, and technical leads who are interested in improving their web API design skills and creating APIs that better serve their business domains
sessions.abstract:
In a world where code is increasingly being generated by AI, the hard work is no longer in writing the code, but in domain discovery, understanding workflows, establishing service boundaries, and supporting personas.

Some may argue that this has always been the case, but it's now becoming more apparent than ever. In order to fully acknowledge this shift, we need to rethink how we design our web APIs. Instead of focusing on the technical aspects of API design, we should focus on the business domain and the behaviors that we want to expose through our APIs.

If you believe that the best, or perhaps even only way to design a web API is around the four verbs of CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete), you are not alone.

While CRUD-based APIs are straightforward and easy to implement, they often fail to capture the true essence of the business domain they are meant to serve. This dissonance between the API design and the business domain can lead to challenges in maintaining and evolving the API as the business requirements change.

This talk explores an alternative approach to designing web APIs that focuses on the behavior in the business domain rather than just the data operations. By leveraging domain-driven design principles, we can create APIs that are more aligned with the business needs, easier to understand, and more adaptable to change.
sessions.title: Domain-driven web APIs
sessions.suggestedKeywords: rest, domain-driven design, web api design, api design, best practices, patterns

speakers.name: Asbjørn Ulsberg
speakers.bio: Software developer hobbyist since 1989, web developer and demoscener since 1996, and professional full-stack developer since 2000, Asbjørn enjoys every aspect of development; from design and architecture to implementation, maintenance and operations. He passionately believes in the free and open web, dabbles in web standards when time permits, and thinks Fielding might be on to something with his 2000 doctoral dissertation. He loves creating and listening to music, tasting whisky and beer, and spending time with his wife and two wonderful daughters.

sessions.intendedAudience: Builders, tech decision makers, devops and SREs.
sessions.abstract: You know that backlog of CVEs, outdated dependencies, and security findings nobody wants to touch? We built an multi-agent orchestrated system to do it for us. kaizen-do runs four scanners in parallel, triages findings with LLMs, and opens pull requests with fixes, with optional human in the loop. It routes high confidence fixes straight to PRs and low confidence ones to issues. It deduplicates across scanners, caps PR volume with backpressure so teams don't drown, and learns from closed PRs to get smarter over time. Built in four weeks by eight engineers from DNB and AWS, with a shared ambition: open source it so any org can point it at their own repos. Go, Step Functions, Bedrock, and a state machine we're oddly proud of. This talk walks through the architecture, the failures that taught us the most, and why a bank and a cloud provider decided the best way to solve this problem was to give the solution away.
sessions.title: kaizen-do: We Built an AI Agent That Maintains 10,000 Repositories (and Only Broke Production Twice)

speakers.name: Yves Hwang
speakers.bio:
25+ years in IT building products, teams, and companies. A strategic thought leader in enterprise settings—and gung-ho enough to have co-founded various startups.

Currently at AWS Norway, where I bring customer obsession, bias for action, and deep enterprise experience to cloud architecture with a startup mentality for product development. AWS certified, open-source contributor, regular speaker at tech conferences, I’m a firm believer that the best solutions come from diverse teams solving hard problems with a Day 1 culture.

speakers.name: Vegard Hansen
speakers.bio: With over a decade of experience in cloud infrastructure as both a developer and architect, I have established myself as a trusted advisor to senior management on cloud adoption. My expertise extends across multiple public cloud providers, with a particular focus on Amazon Web Services and its comprehensive suite of services and tooling.

sessions.intendedAudience: Intermediate to senior Java developers
sessions.abstract:
AI can migrate your thread pools to virtual threads in minutes. It'll even get the tests to pass. But when your service starts leaking security contexts across requests at 3 AM, AI isn't on call. You are.

We'll take a realistic Spring Boot service and systematically move it to virtual threads and structured concurrency through four progressive exercises. But at every step, we'll break things on purpose first: watch ThreadLocal poison contexts between requests, see synchronized blocks pin carrier threads under load, and observe unbounded fan-out overwhelm your database. You'll understand the failure before you write the fix.

Each exercise starts with failing tests that pass when the migration is correct: replacing thread pool executors, rewriting CompletableFuture fan-out with StructuredTaskScope, migrating ThreadLocal to ScopedValue, and diagnosing pinned carriers with JFR. By the end, you won't just have migrated a service. You'll have the mental model to know when something goes wrong in production and why.
sessions.title: AI Can't Debug This: A Virtual Thread Migration Workshop
sessions.suggestedKeywords: Java, Concurrency, Virtual Threads, Workshop, Migration, Spring Boot
sessions.workshopPrerequisites: JDK 21+ (ideally 25), Maven, Docker, and an IDE you're comfortable with.

speakers.name: A N M Bazlur Rahman
speakers.bio:
A N M Bazlur Rahman is a Java Champion and Senior Staff Software Engineer at Hammerspace. He is a Contributing Editor for Java Queue at InfoQ and an internationally recognized conference speaker. Bazlur has over a decade of experience working with Java and related technologies, with a particular focus on concurrency, distributed systems, and modern Java platform features.

He is the author of the O’Reilly book Modern Concurrency in Java, which explores virtual threads, structured concurrency, and the evolving concurrency model of the Java platform. In addition, he has written five programming books in Bengali that have helped thousands of developers learn Java.

Bazlur is also the founder and moderator of the Bangladesh Java User Group (JUG), which he has been leading since 2013 to support and grow the Java community. Through speaking, writing, and mentoring, he actively contributes to the global Java ecosystem and helps developers adopt modern practices and technologies.

sessions.intendedAudience: Developers, data engineers, analytics engineers, technical architects, and engineering leaders evaluating AI initiatives. Attendees will get insights in how focusing on low hanging fruits can often create as much value as intensive AI applications
sessions.abstract:
Across the industry, teams are asking the same question: "What problem can we solve today with AI?" It sounds like the right place to start. In practice, it rarely is.

Again and again, the real answer turns out to be simpler: the problem can already be solved by a trustworthy dashboard that people actually use. The data foundation just was not there yet.

For most organizations, trusted and easy-to-use dashboards cover the majority of real decision needs. AI becomes a high-value addition only after that foundation exists. High-confidence visibility beats low-confidence intelligence.

If your team is asking "What can we solve with AI?" but cannot yet answer "Which KPI can we trust today?", this session is for you. You will leave with a concrete checklist to assess your organization's data readiness and a clear mental model for prioritizing dashboards and data foundations before AI investments.
sessions.title: AI Is Easy. Trustworthy Data Is Hard. Why Data Engineers Matter More Than Ever
sessions.suggestedKeywords: Data, AI, data engineering, data driven decisions

speakers.name: Vegard Sjåvik
speakers.bio: Vegard is a data engineer in Bekk Consulting. He has worked with customers within banking and humanitarian aid to extract value from their data. During his studies of AI at NTNU he specialized in the role of data in AI model training

sessions.intendedAudience: This session is suitable for all audiences, regardless of experience level. You will learn concise coding techniques. The demos are done in Kotlin, but no prior experience is necessary.
sessions.abstract:
Can you create a complete game with just 10 lines of code? That was the quest I set myself when recreating the classic game Snake in as few lines as possible. Let me tell you: I was not ready for the types of challenges I would face. For example, just registering button presses was not as straightforward as I had hoped.

Through live coding, I will show you how I overcame these obstacles and distilled Snake to its absolute essence. What key elements make this game instantly recognizable? By combining Kotlin and Swing, we will end up with a minimal code base and if not award-winning, at least a playable version of Snake.

Striving for conciseness led me to a deeper understanding of the code. I will show how constraints push us to think outside the box and lead to more creative solutions. Moreover, my approach can be applied to any problem. Sometimes the best way to understand code is by seeing how little of it you actually need!
sessions.title: Snake in 10 Lines: Learning More by Coding Less
sessions.suggestedKeywords: Kotlin, clean code, gamification, concise coding

speakers.name: Guus de Wit
speakers.bio: Guus is a Java/Kotlin developer at adesso Netherlands. With a passion for puzzles and a background in science, he strives for elegant solutions to complex problems. In his spare time, he likes to over-optimize the simple tasks of today, to hopefully live a lazy life tomorrow

sessions.intendedAudience: Developers, architects, and team leads who want to know if they're high performing compared to their peers, and what to do if not.
sessions.abstract:
Is your development team working well? How do you know? Teams can easily fall prey to groupthink and convince themselves they are doing great and are high performers. In reality, they're trailing behind without realising they need to up their game. The DevOps Research and Assessment (DORA) have provided a possible antidote, the DORA metrics, that have become an industry standard and can give good insights on how you're doing compared to other developers.

This presentation will give an introduction to the DORA metrics and what they measure. I will then tell about the experiences Stø, the company behind services like BankID and BankAxept, have had so far implementing the metrics in a lightweight way to improve the way we work. You will also hear about other measurements you might consider to get the full picture, and how to avoid metrics gaming. Don't run afoul of Goodheart's law: when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure.
sessions.title: How Are We Doing? Practical Metrics that Matter

speakers.name: Markus Krüger
speakers.bio: Markus Krüger is a developer at Stø AS, the company building and running services like BankID and BankAxept. He has worked as a developer, architect, and tech lead for over 20 years. He has held several presentations at JavaZone and other venues previously, on various subjects such as performance testing, scheduling, scaling systems, cloud architectures, and observability. He likes making large things go fast.

sessions.intendedAudience: Architects, Senior Developers
sessions.abstract: Would you like to move your data and workloads to a European public cloud provider, but don't know where to start? Or did you try to find one, and couldn't decide between too many choices? Join this session and get some practical advice on how to get started, things you should consider before making a decision, and what to expect. And maybe just as important: valid reasons for when European public cloud providers aren't the right choice for you, because like always in our business, “it depends” is the right answer to the question whether European public cloud providers are a fit for your needs.
sessions.title: A Practical Guide to European Public Cloud Providers

speakers.name: Filip Van Laenen
speakers.bio: Filip has over twenty years of experience in the software business. He started as a developer, has been a tech lead and architect, and has spent the last year investigating and talking to European public cloud providers.

sessions.intendedAudience: Utviklere, designere, tech leads og andre som jobber i produktteam og kjenner på friksjonen mellom metodikk på papiret og arbeid i praksis. Foredraget passer for de som har opplevd at prosesser og seremonier tar mer plass enn problemet de prøver å løse, og som vil ha et mer ærlig språk for hvordan team faktisk jobber når virkeligheten ikke passer i malen.
sessions.abstract:
På et eller annet tidspunkt begynte vi å behandle metodikk som religion. Agile ble en dogme, Design Thinking et rituale, og Double Diamond et ikon folk tilber uten å ane hva det egentlig betyr. Alle snakker om å “gjøre det riktig”, men få husker hvorfor vi gjør det i det hele tatt.  

Folk fyller ut canvaser som om de står til eksamen og blir stressa når virkeligheten ikke passer inn i malen. Team sitter fast i seremonier de hater, men tør ikke droppe dem fordi “det står jo i boka”. Når noen endelig spør “gir dette mening?”, blir de sett på som vanskelige.  
  
Det handler ikke om å kaste metodene. De fleste av dem ble tross alt laget for en grunn, men vi må slutte å late som om virkeligheten må bøyes for å passe prosessen. Bruk det som funker, dropp resten, og tenk selv.  
  
Ærlig talt. Ingen av oss driver med ren Agile, ekte Double Diamond, textbook Design Thinking eller hvilken metodikk som tilfeldigvis er in vogue denne måneden. Det er hele poenget.  
  
Keiseren er naken, og noen ganger er det helt all right.
sessions.title: Metodikk som tvangstrøye

speakers.name: Nadia Tokerud
speakers.bio: Nadia jobber til daglig som senior UX-designer i Forte. Hun har lang erfaring fra produktutvikling i tverrfaglige team. Hun har sett på nært hold hva som skjer når team bare funker, og hvordan det kan gå når metodikk blir en tvangstrøye. Hun ser på verden med et pragmatisk blikk og har tro på mantraet "bruk det som funker, skipp resten"

speakers.name: Håvard Haug
speakers.bio: Håvard is a Tech Lead at Plug, where he works to make shipping greener. He has worked with organizations such as the Directorate of Fisheries, TV 2, and Fjordkraft, and previously at Vizrt in Bergen and Zurich. He is passionate about cloud technologies, Kubernetes, and automation.
