sessions.intendedAudience: Utviklere, enten frontend eller backend
sessions.abstract:
Få oversikt over JSON med JSONata-spørringer

Du skal integrere med en ny REST-tjeneste du ikke kjenner fra før. Du kaller et endepunkt i Postman og får noen hundre (eller tusen!) linjer JSON tilbake. Så er det din jobb å finne ut hva alt dette betyr, og hvordan resultatet endrer seg når du kaller tjenesten med litt ulike parametre. 

Å scrolle gjennom store mengder JSON er slitsomt, særlig når du bare er interessert i deler av innholdet. Hva om du kunne skrive en spørring mot JSON-dokumentet for å få litt oversikt? 

I denne lyntalen viser jeg hvordan spørrespråket JSONata lar deg trekke ut deler av et stort JSON-dokument og flate ut dypt nøstede strukturer, slik at du lettere kan forstå innholdet uten å måtte scrolle så mye. Jeg viser hvordan du kan skrive noen små spørringer i en online editor, lime inn et stort dokument og få ut resultater med det samme. 

Jeg viser også hvordan du kan bruke JSONata i koden din, slik at du kan definere dine egne klasser og deserialisere resultatet av JSONata-spørringer i stedet for hele svaret fra en REST-tjeneste. På den måten kan du forenkle og tilpasse strukturen på dataene slik at de passer bedre med problemet du forsøker å løse. Det fører også til enklere kode når du skal bruke dataene.

Målet er at du skal lære nok om JSONata til at du kan prøve det ut selv, slik at du finner ut om det kan være nyttig for deg.
sessions.title: Få oversikt over JSON med JSONata-spørringer

speakers.name: Ove Gram Nipen
speakers.bio: Ove er sjefskonsulent i Kantega, og har 22 års erfaring som utvikler. På fritiden seiler han regatta eller tur med familien. Han er også Taekwon-Do-utøver og -instruktør.

sessions.intendedAudience: Java and Kotlin developers that are using or starting to use AI coding tools
sessions.abstract:
Do you keep the spec around after the agent is done building?

Most of us do not. We write a prompt or a doc to tell the agent what to build, the agent builds it, the spec gets stale, and we move on. Tests come next, written separately. Documentation comes later, if at all. Three artefacts, three formats, three opportunities to drift apart.

There is a better shape. A well-structured table of input-output examples can play all three roles. Before the code exists, the table is a specification — precise enough for an agent to implement against, clear enough for a human to review. As the code takes shape, the same table runs as an executable test, verifying that the implementation matches the spec on every commit. Months later, when someone needs to understand the feature, the table is still there as documentation that cannot drift, because CI fails the moment it does.

We will follow one small feature through this lifecycle using TableTest, an open-source JUnit extension. We will write a table as a spec, hand it to a coding agent, watch the table become a test, and see how the same artefact serves a future developer trying to understand the rules. The takeaway: the artefact you write to tell an agent what to build is the same artefact you would want for review, for verification, and for documentation anyway. Write it once.
sessions.title: Spec, Test, Doc: One Table, Three Lives
sessions.suggestedKeywords: Agentic coding, Spec-driven development, Testing, JUnit, Executable Specifications

speakers.name: Nils Christian Haugen
speakers.bio: Nils Christian loves creating software products. He is a long-time programmer and agile practitioner, combining deep technical skills with solid coaching, leadership, and communicative skills. As a consultant at Scienta, he helps clients deliver software at high pace.

sessions.intendedAudience: People that are interested in the ideas around programming languages. They will see how to get Java and Rust to communicate.
sessions.abstract:
In the evolution of programming languages, with ever-growing codebases, we cannot always justify the cost of rewriting everything into the newest and shiniest language, so programming language interoperability might be a pragmatic solution.
Developing ways for existing programs and libraries to interoperate across programming language barriers is not only a practical necessity: it is a space to engage with the ideas that shape the programming languages we love to use and learn.
Rust and Java are seemingly two radically different languages, although both are hailed as "memory safe". 
So when Java code calls Rust code, and vice versa, you may think you'll have to sacrifice something on either side: the Rust code will become unsafe and dirty in the hands of the JVM, and the Java code will be burdened by the strictness of the borrow checker.

Whether or not that's true, the collision site of the ideas carried by these two languages is fertile ground. 
Both are arriving at the same ideas — memory arenas, explicit lifetime scoping, data-oriented design — from different directions. 
That convergence reflects something larger about how programming languages evolve: not as isolated projects, but as living carriers of ideas that travel across communities, mutate in new contexts, and reshape the languages that inherit them.
This talk explores that convergence through Rust and Java code that interoperates and the ideas that both languages are shaped by.
sessions.title: Rust and Java interoperability - Calling convention as code

speakers.name: Eirik Blekesaune
speakers.bio:
Eirik Blekesaune used to work with art, writing code that made things make noise, light up, and move up and down and around.
Now he is a consultant at KodeWorks, currently enjoying writing Java and TypeScript.
He is especially drawn to thinking and talking about programming languages, how they evolve and what they express.
He is also one of the organizers of the Rust Trondheim meetup group.

sessions.intendedAudience: Java/Kotlin Developers, Platform Engineers, everyone deploying applications on kubernetes
sessions.abstract:
Kubernetes operators have revolutionized how we manage complex, stateful applications on Kubernetes. By encoding domain expertise into custom controllers, operators extend Kubernetes capabilities and automate operational tasks that would otherwise require manual intervention.

In this session, we'll explore the fundamentals of building a Kubernetes operator from scratch. You'll learn how to:

- Understand operator patterns: Discover why operators are essential for managing sophisticated applications and how they leverage Kubernetes' declarative model
- Design custom resources: Learn how to define Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) that model your application's desired state
Implement robust controllers: Build controllers that reconcile the current state with the desired state, handling edge cases and failures gracefully
- Test and deploy: Explore best practices for testing operators and deploying them safely into production environments
sessions.title: Building Production-Ready Kubernetes Operators: A Practical Guide

speakers.name: Frederik Pietzko
speakers.bio:
Frederik started programming in 8th grade, starting out programming Lego Mindstorms and later learning Java and Python for personal Projects.

16 years later Frederik is a Fullstack-Developer with 8 years of professional experience - 4 of those in Kotlin, developing cloud native applications for multiple big customers in the automotive, logistics and energy sector.
During this time he experimented with a lot of different languages and frameworks, developing a broad understanding of the Software development landscape. 
4 years ago he fell in love with Kotlin and has been using it as his primary language since.

In his free time he likes to explore programming langues and frameworks, meet and chat to other software enthusiasts and play with his cats.

sessions.intendedAudience: Anyone who likes fun and out of the box projects and/or people who want to learn more about spring internals.
sessions.abstract:
If you work on enterprise grade microservices on the JVM, you have undoubtedly heard of Spring Boot. Most developers see it as the reliable backbone of enterprise software. It is stable, predictable, and safe. But what if we took all those conventions and threw them out the window? What if we repurposed the IoC container, event listeners, and bean lifecycles to reveal what Spring Boot was always meant to be? A game development framework!

Join Alexander as he attempts to deconstruct and demystify the Spring ecosystem by forcing it to do things it was definitely not designed for. We are not talking about simple text adventures here. We are talking about building complex, real-time games like a Terraria clone or a souls-like RPG, all powered by the same annotations you use for your day job.

This session explores what happens when you learn technologies by breaking them. Whether you are a Spring veteran or a newcomer, this talk offers a fresh perspective on the tools you use every day. You will leave with a better understanding of how Spring Boot works under the hood and the inspiration to build something completely unnecessary, just because you can. Come for the absurdity, stay for the laughs, and see what happens when you strip away conventions and best practices in the pursuit of a wild idea.
sessions.title: Let’s use Spring Boot to build games, because why not?

speakers.name: Alexander Chatzizacharias
speakers.bio: Alexander, a 35-year-old Software Engineer at JDriven, holds dual Dutch and Greek nationality. He earned his master’s degree in Game Studies from the University of Amsterdam, where he discovered his passion for gamification and software engineering. Alexander aims to bridge the gap between game development and software engineering, believing that both industries have much to learn from each other. He is dedicated to integrating technologies and methodologies from both fields. Additionally, he enjoys experimenting with new technologies and cutting-edge sdk's.

sessions.intendedAudience: Alle som jobber med utvikling av applikasjoner, spesielt frontend. Fint å ha vært borti react/annen frontend.
sessions.abstract:
Visste du at testdrevet utvikling (TDD) ikke bare er for backend? I dette foredraget viser jeg hvordan du kan refaktorere en React-applikasjon fra monolittiske komponenter til modulære byggeklosser, og hvordan denne arkitekturen åpner døren for TDD i frontend.

Foredraget er todelt. Først ser vi på hvordan separation of concerns og custom hooks lar deg dele opp frontend-koden i små, uavhengige moduler som er enkle å forstå, teste og gjenbruke. Deretter tar vi steget videre og ser på hvordan denne strukturen gjør det mulig å jobbe testdrevet. Red, green, refactor.

Alt vises live med en ekte React-applikasjon som refaktoreres steg for steg, fra “alt i én komponent” til en ren, modulær arkitektur med tester som dokumentasjon.
sessions.title: Fra spagetti til legoklosser: Modularisert arkitektur og TDD i frontend

speakers.name: Andrea Magnussen
speakers.bio: Andrea er fagleder og utvikler i utvikleravdelingen i Sparebank 1 Utvikling og brenner for å redusere den kognitive lasten som trengs for å lage gode produkter, spesielt i frontend. TDD FTW!

sessions.intendedAudience: developers, architects
sessions.abstract:
Writing a "Hello World" endpoint is easy. But building an API that is secure, observable, and capable of evolving without breaking every client? That’s where the real engineering happens. We often pollute our business logic with validation, security checks, and boilerplate, creating a maintenance nightmare.

It’s time to clean up our act. In this session, we’ll move beyond basic CRUD to explore 5 essential patterns for building production-grade APIs. We will combine standard Jakarta EE/MicroProfile features with powerful custom implementations to tackle the cross-cutting concerns that matter most.
sessions.title: An Opinionated Guide to Bulletproof APIs with Java
sessions.suggestedKeywords: API design, REST, architecture, Java, JakartaEE

speakers.name: Rustam Mehmandarov
speakers.bio: Passionate computer scientist. Java Champion, Google Developers Expert for Cloud, and Docker Captain. Public speaker. Community builder. Mentor.

sessions.intendedAudience: Everyone who writes software
sessions.abstract:
Semantic versioning? Calendar versioning? Or do we need to bother with versions at all? No matter which you choose, they all bring their own possibilities and problems. Choose the wrong one, and you might end up dealing with issues that could be avoided altogether - for no actual benefit!

Let's get systematic, and walk through the pros and cons of each variant, so that we're all better equipped to tackle this choice the next time it comes up!
sessions.title: How do we version software?

speakers.name: Øystein Hagen Blixhavn
speakers.bio:
Øystein Hagen Blixhavn is passionate about getting stuff done, in basically all senses of the words. If it reduces wasteful effort and time, fantastic. If it improves the developer experience and increases their flow, amazing! This aspiration has led him to roles as system- and platform architect and platform developer, because these roles are directly concerned with increasing rate of stuff getting done.

Learning stuff and gaining cool insights is another thing that drives him, and his talks always seek to stimulate curiosity and induce this feeling in the audience.

sessions.intendedAudience: Software Developers, DevOps People, and everyone else interested in how to simplify their container setup
sessions.abstract:
Cloud Native Buildpacks make it easier for developers to create container images, offering a seamless experience with built-in features such as rebasing, reproducibility, and support for multiple entry points. In this talk, we'll start by covering the basics of Buildpacks, looking at their architecture and the benefits they bring compared to traditional methods of building container images.

We’ll then explore the lifecycle component, which is the central binary of Cloud Native Buildpacks. This part will reveal how it manages the conversion of source code into OCI images and oversees the various stages of the build process, including detection, installation, and export.

We will also discuss the Paketo Buildpacks project, emphasizing its flexibility and community focus. This section will show how Paketo improves the developer experience by offering a curated selection of buildpacks that make configuration easier and enhance performance. Additionally, we'll share real-world examples of how organizations are using Buildpacks to streamline their CI/CD pipelines and speed up their deployment cycles. To wrap up, we’ll go over some best practices for integrating Buildpacks into your workflows, providing tips on maximizing their features for better performance and security.
sessions.title: Understanding Buildpacks: Delving Deep into Their Functionality

speakers.name: Patrick Baumgartner
speakers.bio:
Patrick Baumgartner is a Java Champion, passionate software crafter, and technical agile coach at 42talents. He helps teams build elegant, simple, and robust solutions, specializing in cloud software with Java, the Spring ecosystem, and other open-source technologies.

Patrick is an active member of the Software Craft, Java, and Agile communities in Switzerland. As a trainer and coach, he focuses on making a real impact by sharing knowledge, experimenting, and fostering continuous improvement. He enjoys learning with and from others.

sessions.intendedAudience: software developers interested in seeing how they can create intelligent agentic systems for a real use case, platform engineers and SRE who would like to reduce their MTTR
sessions.abstract:
Your software rollouts to production are probably always flawless, right? For the rest of us,  even with robust CI/CD once in a while we do run into issues when releasing code to production. Now even more so with more and more code written by AI assistants. 

In Kubernetes, the Argo Rollouts project excels at Progressive Delivery and automated rollbacks to mitigate deployment issues, but what if we could go a step further, and perhaps add a touch of Agentic Java to the mix?

This session explores how to elevate your release process by integrating Agentic AI (written in Java) and asynchronous coding agents, with Argo Rollouts canary deployments. We'll demonstrate how a intelligent, Java based agents can automatically analyze a rollout failure, pinpointing the root cause. Beyond diagnosis, these agents can take proactive steps on your behalf, suggesting and even implementing code fixes as new pull requests, which can be redeployed automatically after PR review. This approach moves us closer to truly self-healing deployments.

Join us to learn how to combine the power of Kubernetes and Argo Rollouts with the autonomous capabilities of LangChain4j agentic, achieving a release experience that is not only seamless but also resilient.
sessions.title: Self-Healing Rollouts: Automating Production Fixes with Agentic AI

speakers.name: Kevin Dubois
speakers.bio: Kevin Dubois is a software architect and platform engineer with a career spanning over 20 years. He is often featured as a keynote speaker at conferences around the world where he shares his experience and knowledge about cloud native & AI software development, developer experience, open source and Java. Kevin is also an author and Java Champion. He currently works as a Senior Principal Developer Advocate at IBM, and is also Technical Lead for the CNCF Developer Experience Technical Advisory Group.

speakers.name: Carlos Sanchez
speakers.bio: Carlos Sanchez is a Principal Scientist at Adobe Experience Manager, specializing in software automation, from build tools to Continuous Delivery and Progressive Delivery. Involved in Open Source for over 20 years, he is the author of the Jenkins Kubernetes plugin and a member of the Apache Software Foundation amongst other open source groups, contributing to several projects, such as Kubernetes, Jenkins or Apache Maven.

sessions.intendedAudience: developers using AI coding assistants, platfrom teams enabling AI for the company
sessions.abstract:
Letting an AI agent loose for solving development tasks is a productivity dream, until it decides to optimise your home directory or brick your system by upgrading Python. We want that YOLO mode speed, but without the security nightmares. 

In this session, we’ll look at Docker Sandboxes: a new primitive designed to let agents operate in a restricted cocoon with limited access to the filesystem and controlled network and secret injections. We’ll dive into the typical mess AI agents create, see why basic isolation isn't enough, and walk through a workflow for running agents that you can actually trust.
sessions.title: You're absolutely right, it was your home directory!

speakers.name: Oleg Šelajev
speakers.bio:
AI and Developer relations at Docker

Oleg Šelajev is a developer advocate at Docker working on AI, developer productivity, Testcontainers. Developer. Author. Speaker. Java Champion, Microsoft MVP, Docker captain.

Oleg is a seasoned open source contributor and loves herding agents for building random software here and there.

sessions.intendedAudience: Utviklere, arkitekter
sessions.abstract: Regulære uttrykk (Regex’er) er et nyttig verktøy som brukes overalt til mye forskjellig. Men visste du at den enkle, korte Regex’en din er en potensiell tikkende CPU-bombe? I denne lyntalen deler jeg vår tabbe med en uskyldig Regex som førte til bom stopp i produksjon hos en stort norsk kringkastingselskap…
sessions.title: Hvorfor du bør ha timeout i Regex’en din…

speakers.name: Stian Gjedrem
speakers.bio: Stian is an experienced software developer and has been part of Boitano since its birth in 2018. Stian is particularly happy when he can create user-friendly software that people love to use!

sessions.intendedAudience: Alle som koder
sessions.abstract:
Smidig utvikling var et svar på et konkret problem. Krav kan ikke spesifiseres presist i forkant fordi forståelsen av problemet utvikler seg underveis. Iterasjon, feedback og brukerhistorier var løsningen på nettopp dette.

KI-assistert utvikling endrer forutsetningene. Når implementasjonstiden komprimeres, flyttes flaskehalsen til spesifisering. Presise instruksjoner gir presise resultater. Den iterative oppdagelsesprosessen smidig bygger på, erstattes gradvis av noe som strukturelt ligner kravspesifikasjon. Vi kaller det bare prompt engineering.

Denne lyntalen tar for seg denne erkjennelsen og diskuterer konsekvensene det har for utvikling, team, leveranse og prosess. Til slutt besvarer vi det store spørsmålet:

Er smidig død?
sessions.title: Fossefall vant og vi kaller det prompting

speakers.name: Nora Gjøen-Gjøsæter
speakers.bio:
Nora Gjøen-Gjøsæter er avdelingsleder og KI-lead i Kantega Bergen, med over åtte års hands-on erfaring fra data. og maskinlæringsprosjekt. Hun har ledet og bidratt til komplekse prosjekter i en lang rekke bransjer, fra forsikring og offentlig sektor til startups, og fra storstilte dataplattformer til lettbente KI-løsninger i produksjon.

Nora er programkomitéleder for Make Data Smart-konferansen og er en aktiv stemme i det norske tech-miljøet. Med bakgrunn som fotballspiller på toppnivå tar hun med seg et tydelig fokus på prestasjon og robuste team inn i teknologifaget.

sessions.intendedAudience: Java developers, Maven users (beginner/intermediate)
sessions.abstract:
Maven is an ubiquitous build tool in the Java ecosystem, some even claim it’s the de facto standard build tool. Configuring Maven is deceptively simple, after all it’s just a matter of writing XML, isn’t it? Things look differently when the rubber meets the road. One must know the intricacies of the build lifecycle; how plugins, goals (mojos), and phases come together; rules for dependency resolution; configuration inheritance between parent – child POM files; enhancing the build with profiles; and more. That’s a lot to keep track of. Fortunately modern IDEs like IntelliJ IDEA provide great support to configure and run Maven, with code completion, navigation, custom run configurations, and more.

Come learn how both tools can be used together for better results in your daily work.
sessions.title: Getting more out of Maven
sessions.suggestedKeywords: Maven, build tools, dependencies, IDE, IntelliJ IDEA

speakers.name: Marit van Dijk
speakers.bio:
With over 20 years of software development experience, Marit is passionate about building great software with great people, and making developers’ lives easier.
She regularly presents at international conferences and shares her expertise through webinars, podcasts, blog posts, videos, and tutorials. She's also contributed to the book “97 Things Every Java Programmer Should Know” (O’Reilly Media).

speakers.name: Andres Almiray
speakers.bio: Andres is a Java/Groovy developer and a Java Champion with more than 20 years of experience in software design and development. He has been involved in web and desktop application development since the early days of Java. Andres is a true believer in open source and has participated on popular projects like Groovy, Griffon, and DbUnit, as well as starting his own projects. Founding member of the Griffon framework and Hackergarten community event. Creator of JReleaser.

sessions.intendedAudience: Utviklere og arkitekter uavhengig av erfaringsnivå
sessions.abstract:
Hvordan organiserer du koden din i pakker/moduler?

Da jeg begynte å utvikle software profesjonelt i 2001 lærte jeg å organisere koden i tekniske lag; package-by-layer. Dette opplevde jeg lenge som en bransjestandard, men rundt 2010 begynte jeg å utforske andre måter å organisere koden jeg jobbet på. Da oppdaget jeg at det er flere måter å gjøre dette på.

Jeg har gjort meg en god del gode erfaringer, men jeg har også gjort feil på veien som jeg ønsker å dele.

Mitt største ønske er å skape nysgjerrighet for å utforske andre måter å designe software på, samtidig som jeg er klar over at dette heller ikke er noen fasit.

Jeg har også erfart at hvordan jeg deler bort, ikke alltid treffer like godt. Jeg forsøker å lære av det, som jeg lærer av software og prøve nye angrepsvinkler. Derfor vil jeg denne gangen starte med å fortelle min historie og hvilke problemet det har løst, før jeg snakker om løsning.
sessions.title: Package-by-(sub)domain

speakers.name: Terje Heen
speakers.bio: Terje jobber som Principal Software Engineer i Telenor. Han har tidligere jobbet som konsulent i 15 år, og Nav i 8 år.

sessions.intendedAudience: Team som jobber med testautomatisering, og som ønsker å forstå hvor testeren skaper mest verdi når de repeterbare regresjonstestene i større grad blir automatisert.
sessions.abstract:
Mange team automatiserer regresjonstestene sine for å få raskere tilbakemeldinger og mindre manuelt arbeid. Det er et viktig steg. Men når de repeterbare kontrollene blir tatt av verktøyene, oppstår et nytt spørsmål: Hvor skaper testeren mest verdi da?

I denne lyntalen utforsker jeg hvordan testerrollen endrer seg når regresjonen blir automatisert. Jeg viser hvorfor automatisering ikke reduserer behovet for testfaglig kompetanse, men tvert imot gjør den enda viktigere i andre deler av utviklingen. Når testeren får mindre tid bundet opp i faste sjekker, kan mer energi brukes på risikovurdering, utforskende testing, kvalitet i krav, testdesign og læring i teamet.

Dette er en lyntale for team og organisasjoner som vil lykkes med testautomatisering uten å miste blikket for hvor kvalitet faktisk skapes.
sessions.title: Når regresjonen blir automatisert: hvor skaper testeren mest verdi?

speakers.name: Gerd Stalheim Wiggen
speakers.bio: Jeg har over 18 års erfaring fra teknologi‑ og kvalitetsarbeid, med roller som utvikler, tester og testleder. Jeg brenner for hva testing egentlig er – ikke som en sjekklisteaktivitet, men som en måte å forstå risiko, bygge kvalitet og styrke teamenes beslutninger. Hvordan testerne bidrar til verdi i utviklingsteam, utover å "klikke rundt" engasjerer meg. Akkurat nå er jeg særlig opptatt av hvordan god testpraksis og testautomatisering blir en naturlig del av kultur, samarbeid og produktutvikling. Det handler om å gi mennesker mer tid til å gjøre det mennesker gjør best, og hvordan kvalitet skapes sammen og ikke automatisk.

sessions.intendedAudience: Hvis du kan litt JavaScript eller ønsker å følge med på det nyeste.
sessions.abstract: Hvert år kommer ny funksjonalitet til språket JavaScript. La meg gå gjennom hva som er nytt i 2026.
sessions.title: Nyheter i JavaScript - ES2026

speakers.name: Gaute Meek Olsen
speakers.bio: Gaute er en utvikler med ekstra lidenskap for frontend. Gaute er konsulent i Capra og har som regel et hobby prosjekt på siden og liker å holde seg oppdatert innenfor programmering.

sessions.intendedAudience: No experience needed or some familiarity with what LLMs are. Anyone interested in robotics and microcontrollers, anyone curious about what's the next big thing
sessions.abstract:
Are you tired of the same old AI, LLM, buzzword sessions and wondering if there is still anything new and cool out there we can do with AI? Do you crave an exciting, slightly ridiculous project that's just a lot of fun - while also learning about a whole new field with lots of promise? Don't miss this session!
Physical AI is about bringing together all the amazing breakthroughs (yes, like large language models) into the fields of robotics and embedded systems. We can make autonomous robots and systems that can generate their own movement instructions and evolve, without having to painstakingly define all the complex code or even tiny physics-engine implementations to get going.
But first - we need to build actual robots! As someone coming from the data science field - this may sound like a bit of a leap.
However, with a "I'm sure it can't be that hard" attitude, a bunch of electronics components, and an uncontainable enthusiasm - I set out to do just that.
I will share the story of building my own Robot Arm from 0 to an incrementally more complex prototype, and how I started giving it some "AI superpowers", and what's next in this exciting field!
sessions.title: How I built my own intelligent Robot Arm from Scratch

speakers.name: Iulia Feroli
speakers.bio:
Iulia’s mission is to make tech exciting, understandable, and accessible to the new generation.
With a background spanning data science, AI, cloud architecture, and open source she brings a unique perspective on bridging technical depth with approachability.
She’s building her own brand, Back To Engineering, through which she creates a community for tech enthusiasts, engineers, and makers. From YouTube videos on building robots from scratch, to conference talks or keynotes about real, grounded AI, and technical blogs & tutorials — Iulia shares her message worldwide on how to turn complex concepts into tools developers can use every day.

sessions.intendedAudience: krever ingen forkunnskaper, og er for alle som jobber i utviklingsteam
sessions.abstract: En reise gjennom utfordringene og frustrasjonene ved å være utvikler i et produktteam i en ikke-produktorientert organisasjon. Hva skjer når man blir kastet inn i et prosjekt der en ferdig løsning blir kjøpt inn for å erstatte et kritisk delsystem – uten at man har fullført jobben med å forstå de eksisterende prosessene eller integrasjonene? Dette foredraget dykker ned i de konkrete problemene som oppsto, hvordan vi håndterte dem, og de lærerike erfaringene vi sitter igjen med etter å ha jobbet med et produkt som skulle løse alt, men i stedet skapte mer kaos.
sessions.title: Conways lov i praksis

speakers.name: Oda Enger Hoem
speakers.bio: Oda jobber som softwareutvikler og foretrekker backend java. Hun er nysgjerrig på ny teknologi og har et ønske om å alltid lære mer. I team trives hun best når kode og arkitektur diskuteres slik at vi sammen finner den beste løsningen.

sessions.intendedAudience: Utviklere
sessions.abstract: En mission for å forenkle oppsettet på prosjektet førte til en innsikt om at vi kan gjenbruke hvordan docker gjør authentication og bruke dette når vi skal gjøre maven repository authentication.
sessions.title: En bedre maven authentication

speakers.name: Erlend Hamnaberg
speakers.bio: Erlend er en konsultent i Arktekk. Han arbeider for tiden for Sikt Kunnskapssektorens tjenesteleverandør med devops og utvikleropplevelse.

sessions.intendedAudience: The session is recommended for all curious people who want to broaden their knowledge about the art of programming. Experience level - beginners and above.
sessions.abstract:
Since the dawn of computing, we have strived to command machines as efficiently as possible. Over the last century, a myriad of programming languages has emerged, transforming how we converse with silicon. We have tested and implemented new concepts, paradigms, and patterns.

However, it increasingly feels like we are moving in circles, revisiting ideas pioneered in decades past. It is time to pause and reflect on the state of the programmer's primary arsenal.

In this session, we will tackle the hard questions:
- Are we stuck in a loop of reinventing the wheel?
- Are there deep analogies between code and natural languages?
- Why do some languages thrive for decades while others end up in the graveyard of history?
- Why do we get so attached to specific tools while despising alternatives?
- Do we prefer writing yet another CRUD in the latest Java, or sending humans to space using "obsolete" tech?
- Will English become the ultimate programming language in the AI era?
sessions.title: Talking to Machines: The History and Future of Programming Languages

speakers.name: Piotr Łaskawiec
speakers.bio: Pragmatic software architect and common sense evangelist. Deeply believes in holistic approach to software development. Occasional speaker and trainer. JVM enthusiast. Mastering the craft since the times of C64. He thrives on helping others to deliver high-quality solutions. Founder of Comsenity.

sessions.intendedAudience: Utviklere, datafolk, prosjektledere
sessions.abstract:
Har du lyst til å bli datadreven, men opplever at avstanden mellom idé og gjennomføring er for stor?

I SAMT-BU-prosjektet jobbar vi med å redusere denne avstanden ved å bygge bru mellom utviklarar og datafolk – og mellom proprietære løysingar og åpne standardar. Målet er ikkje berre betre system, men betre flyt: i utvikling, integrasjon og samarbeid på tvers av kommunar.

I dette foredraget deler vi korleis vi nyttar opensource-verktøy, felles standardar og ein bevisst samarbeidskultur for å skape fart og gjenbruk. Du får konkrete erfaringar, praktiske grep og ein verktøykasse du kan ta med deg vidare – uavhengig av om du jobbar teknisk, strategisk eller operativt.
sessions.title: Når verktøyet er meir enn halve jobben – korleis opensource, åpne standardar og samarbeid øker digitaliseringa i kommune-Norge

speakers.name: Audun Vindenes Egge
speakers.bio: Audun har jobba i skjæringspunktet mellom utvikling, data, smidig metode og ledelse. Han brenn for å skalere verdiskapinga gjennom åpne standarder, gode verktøy og å gi tillit til medarbeiderne. Dersom alle kunne gjort jobben til ein kollega litt enklare ville vi få ein positiv spiral og bli ustoppelige!

speakers.name: Erik Hagen
speakers.bio: Sett inn bio her

sessions.intendedAudience: Developers interested in Robotics
sessions.abstract:
Has this ever happened to you: You just spent 50k€ on a humanoid robot but it's not very fun at parties so you have to train it to dance using reinforcement learning in a simulation environment on your 100k€ GPU Cluster? What do you mean "NO"?

In this talk, we will teach you how you can train your humanoid robot to dance using reinforcement learning in simulation environments. We're gonna kick things off with an introduction into Reinforcement Learning (RL), covering the basics of policies and rewards. After that, we'll take our first steps in simulation environments with the introduction of NVIDIA Isaac Sim and the Unitree RL Lab. We will run through the steps of shaping a walking policy, one hilarious fall at a time. Finally, we're going to shake things up with a dancing policy: You will learn how you can retarget human motion from videos to the Unitree G1 robot and train an imitation policy that can dance just as poorly as you. 

Throughout the talk, we will do a live training of a walking policy on stage, starting early on in the presentation and checking up on the progress from time to time. For the grande finale, our humanoid robot will close the session with a dancing performance, not missing out on a single step.
sessions.title: Let's Dance! - Teaching your Robot some Moves with Reinforcement Learning

speakers.name: Thomas Endres
speakers.bio:
Thomas Endres is a Managing Partner for TNG Technology Consulting in Munich. Besides his normal work for the company and the management of the customers he is creating various prototypes, including real-time deepfakes, an AI for generating presentations or various robotics showcases. He works on applications in the field of AI, robotics, AR/VR and teleoperation. Furthermore, he is involved in Open Source projects.

Thomas studied computer science at TU Munich and is a passionate software developer. As an Intel Software Innovator and Black Belt, he presents new technologies such as AI, AR/VR, and robotics worldwide. For this, he has received awards including a JavaOne Rockstar Award and several Best Speaker Awards.
